paper_title: AI scientists produce results without reasoning scientifically

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language model (LLM)-based systems are increasingly deployed to conduct scientific research autonomously, yet whether their reasoning adheres to the epistemic norms that make scientific inquiry self-correcting is poorly understood. Here, we evaluate LLM-based scientific agents across eight domains, spanning workflow execution to hypothesis-driven inquiry, through more than 25,000 agent runs and two complementary lenses: (i) a systematic performance analysis that decomposes the contributions of the base model and the agent scaffold, and (ii) a behavioral analysis of the epistemological structure of agent reasoning. We observe that the base model is the primary determinant of both performance and behavior, accounting for 41.4% of explained variance versus 1.5% for the scaffold. Across all configurations, evidence is ignored in 68% of traces, refutation-driven belief revision occurs in 26%, and convergent multi-test evidence is rare. The same reasoning pattern appears whether the agent executes a computational workflow or conducts hypothesis-driven inquiry. They persist even when agents receive near-complete successful reasoning trajectories as context, and the resulting unreliability compounds across repeated trials in epistemically demanding domains. Thus, current LLM-based agents execute scientific workflows but do not exhibit the epistemic patterns that characterize scientific reasoning. Outcome-based evaluation cannot detect these failures, and scaffold engineering alone cannot repair them. Until reasoning itself becomes a training target, the scientific knowledge produced by such agents cannot be justified by the process that generated it.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: DENDRAL,1 one of the first artificial intelligence (AI) systems to produce scientific result, showed its reasoning at every step. Developed by Feigenbaum, Lederberg, and Djerassi in 1965, it inferred molecular structures from mass spectra by generating candidate structures, matching them against spectral evidence, and eliminating those that failed. DENDRAL established template for AI in science: epistemic machinery encoded in explicit rules, every inference step inspectable. Expert systems built on this template remain in use in laboratories today. Large language models (LLMs) have renewed this ambition. growing number of LLM-based agents are now designed to conduct scientific research autonomously, from hypothesis generation through experimentation to manuscript writing (see Section A.6 for the rising interest in those systems).29 Yet the nature of these systems differs fundamentally from their predecessors. Where DENDRAL showed its reasoning at every step, LLM-based agents do not. Their behavior derives from statistical regularities learned during self-supervised training. The epistemic process by which they arrive at scientific conclusions is largely inaccessible to scrutiny. This opacity matters for science itself. The tools scientists use shape the science they produce.10 Evans and colleagues recently confirmed this for AI: the adoption of machine learning (ML) is narrowing the hypotheses scientists pursue.11 LLM-based agents go further, directing the inquiry itself. How these systems reason, and whether their reasoning adheres to the norms that make scientific inquiry self-correcting, spans computer science, philosophy of science, and metascience.12,13 It bears on whether AI systems can produce scientific understanding beyond correct predictions.14 Current evaluations do not address this. Benchmarks for scientific agents measure task completion: whether system answers domain-specific questions,1517 executes data-analysis or discovery workflow,9,1820 reimplements published result, or produces manuscript that passes peer review.2125 These metrics are task-specific. They reveal whether an agent succeeded but not how or why, and they do not predict behavior on new problems.26 The epistemic process underlying result determines whether the knowledge produced is justified in the philosophical sense of justified true belief,27 whether failures are predictable, and whether the system can be trusted beyond the conditions under which it was tested. Here, we study LLM-based scientific agents, also referred to as AI scientists, through two complementary lenses. The first is systematic performance evaluation across controlled environments with standardized tools, graded task scopes, and fine-grained subtask diagnostics, designed to separate the contributions of the base model from the agent scaffold.28 The second is behavioral analysis of the epistemological structure of agent reasoning: what cognitive operations an agent performs, how they relate to one another, and whether they constitute disciplined inquiry. We evaluate three frontier models paired with two scaffold architectures across eight scientific domains, from molecular simulation and surface construction to spectroscopic structure elucidation and qualitative chemical analysis (see Figure 1). We find that the base model is the primary determinant of both performance and behavior, with the scaffold contributing little. Agents routinely ignore evidence they have gathered, commit to hypotheses without testing them, and fail to revise beliefs when confronted with contradictory data. These patterns do not adapt to the demands of the problem. The same undisciplined reasoning appears whether the task requires executing computational workflow or conducting hypothesis-driven inquiry under uncertainty. These findings suggest that evaluating LLM-based agents requires direct assessment of their reasoning process, and that progress will require changes at the base-model level rather than further scaffold engineering.

sections.title: 2 Results
sections.content: We model LLM-based agents as language decision processes,29,30 in which composite policy factors into base-model component governing token-level choices and scaffold component governing prompting strategy, tool routing, and orchestration logic (Figure 1A, Methods Section 4.1). In practice, scaffold wraps the base model in loop: it formats prompts, routes tool calls, appends observations to the conversation history, and repeats until termination criterion is reached. Common implementations include ReAct31 and structured tool-calling interfaces. This decomposition allows us to attribute observed behavior to the base model or to the scaffold. We evaluate agents through two lenses: systematic performance analysis supported by manually curated diagnostic assessments, and behavioral analysis of the epistemological structure of reasoning traces. We constructed environments in eight scientific domains that vary along two dimensions: epistemic demand and task scope (Figure 1D, Section 4.4). Epistemic demand captures the kind of reasoning problem requires, ranging from workflow to hypothesis-driven tasks. Workflow-construction tasks include molecular simulation, surface construction, machine learning, and atomic force microscopy, which are procedurally complex but epistemically straightforwardthe solution path is well-defined and the agent must execute it correctly. Hypothesis-driven tasks in spectroscopic structure elucidation, qualitative chemical analysis, and circuit inference are different. The agent must first understand the problem space itself, then form hypotheses about hidden structure, design discriminating experiments, and revise beliefs as evidence accumulates. Retrosynthetic planning occupies an intermediate position, requiring strategic search through large combinatorial spaces under cost and availability constraints. Problem scope varies within each domain through graded configurations that expand problem scope by modulating the search space: from few to many interacting ions, from simple to complex resistor networks, from spectral confirmation to full elucidation, from measuring friction to (re)discovering power-law behavior. Each environment further decomposes into subtasks isolating specific cognitive operationsretrieval, execution, reasoning, and validation (more details in Section 4.2). 3 Figure 1: Benchmarking scientific reasoning across epistemic demand and problem scope. (A) Agents as language decision processes. base LLM policy is augmented with an agent scaffold (e.g., reasoning, reflection, tool use) to form an agent that interacts with scientific environments via iterative act-observe loops. (B) Large-scale evaluation grounds our findings. The benchmark comprises 25,000+ agent runs across 15+ environment scopes and 90+ tools, enabling statistically grounded evaluation of model and scaffold contributions. (C) Mechanistic and epistemological analyses reveal failure modes. We analyze agent behavior through mechanistic analysis of trajectories and epistemological analysis of reasoning processes. (D) Environments are organized along an axis of increasing epistemic demand (left to right). Workflow execution tasks (e.g., Molecular Simulation, Adsorption Surface Construction, ML-based Property Prediction, AFM Experiment Execution) emphasize procedural correctness; strategic reasoning tasks (e.g., Retrosynthetic Planning) require multi-step planning under constraints; hypothesis-driven inquiry tasks (e.g., Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation, Inorganic Qualitative Analysis, Circuit Inference) require generating, testing, and revising competing hypotheses. Within each domain, task scope increases through graded configurations (sn sn+1) that expand the search space. Axis labels WE and mark workflow-execution and evidence anchors; H, G, and mark hypothesis, goal, and protocol anchors; T, J, and denote test, judge, and update operations. 4 Within each group, specific environments were designed to isolate aspects of scientific reasoning that are difficult to probe with existing benchmarks. The qualitative analysis environment simulates chemical equilibria from thermodynamic data, requiring iterative experimental design under limited sample volume. The circuit inference environment isolates logical reasoning from domain knowledge. The spectroscopic structure elucidation environment poses the same class of problem that motivated DENDRALinferring molecular identity from spectral evidencewith access to multiple analytical techniques and costinformation tradeoff. All environments share common implementation framework with standardized tool interfaces and can be explored interactively at https://lamalab-org. github.io/corral/#environments.

sections.title: The base model determines performance
sections.content: We evaluated three frontier (two commercial closed-source and one open-source) models GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06), Claude Sonnet 4.5 (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929), and GPT-OSS (gpt-oss-120b)each paired with two scaffold architectures (ReAct and structured tool-calling), across all eight domains and their task scopes (Figure 2; per-configuration scores in Tables G.8 and G.9). Agents solve workflow-construction tasks at guided scopes reliably: in molecular simulation and surface construction, leading configurations approach the performance ceiling (see Figure 2). Performance declines as the problem scope broadens within domain and declines more steeply as epistemic demand increases. In Inorganic Qualitative Analysis and Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation at the broadest scope, even the strongest configuration falls below 60%. Across nearly all environments, the performance spread attributable to model choice exceeds the spread attributable to scaffold choice (Figure 2C). Both scaffolds yield the same decline in Pass@5 from retrieval through execution and reasoning to validation (Figure 2B). Manual annotation of reasoning traces by two expert annotators, using custom tool and taxonomy developed for this study (Section F), independently confirms this pattern (Extended Data Figure 2). To move beyond task-level performance and identify the underlying capabilities that drive it, we constructed diagnostic battery of question-answer pairs for each domain, separately targeting the knowledge and reasoning operations associated with each environment (Section 4.6). We applied item response theory (IRT) to these diagnostic items.32,33 IRT jointly estimates item difficulty and respondent ability from patterns of correct and incorrect answers, yielding latent ability parameters for each model-environment combination (Figure 3A, Section G.4). The resulting capability profiles differ across models (Figure 3B). The separation is most pronounced for reasoning ability in hypothesis-driven domains, where the gap between the strongest and weakest model exceeds 5.3 standard units in Inorganic Qualitative Analysis (see Figure G.6 for all environments). In Retrosynthetic Planning and Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation, two of the three models effectively lack the domain knowledge required. The ML-based Property Prediction environment shows the smallest 5 Figure 2: Performance is primarily driven by model choice and degrades with epistemic demand. (A) Heatmap of average scores for all model-scaffold configurations across environment-scope combinations, grouped by epistemic demand category. (B) Average Pass@5 across subtask categories ordered by increasing cognitive complexity. Both scaffolds exhibit the same decline from retrieval to validation. (C) Model spread (performance range across models) versus scaffold spread (performance range across scaffolds) for each environment. Nearly all environments fall above the diagonal, indicating that model choice, not scaffold design, is the primary source of performance variation. Figure 3: Reasoning ability is the dominant predictor of task success. (A) twostage latent-factor framework separates knowledge and reasoning contributions to task success. Stage 1: item response theory estimates latent knowledge and reasoning abilities from domain-specific diagnostic assessments. Stage 2: prediction model combines these ability estimates with environment, scope, scaffold, and verbosity to predict task success probability. (B) Models diverge most in hypothesis-driven domains. Reasoning capability profiles show the largest separation in Inorganic Qualitative Analysis and Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation. (C) Reasoning ability dominates; scaffold choice is negligible. Variance decomposition of the prediction model: reasoning ability (41.4%) and environmentscope interaction (30.1%) together account for over 70% of explained variance. Scaffold (1.5%) and verbosity (0.1%) contribute minimally. knowledge gap (0.62 standard units, see Figure G.6 for knowledge capabilities), consistent with the broad coverage of machine-learning topics in LLM training data. We combined these IRT-derived abilities with environment, scope, scaffold, and verbositylevel (see Methods for details) as parameters in latent factor model predicting task success (Figure 3A). We evaluated eight candidate model specifications of increasing structural complexity (M1M8; Section G.5.1) and selected the best-fitting model (M7) by PSIS-LOO cross-validation (Section G.5, Figure G.9). The variance decomposition is clear (Figure 3C). Reasoning ability accounts for 41.4% of the explained variance, environment scope for 30.1%, scaffold for 1.5% and verbosity for 0.1%. All three independent analyses (raw performance comparisons, manual trace annotation, and latent factor modeling) converge on the same conclusion: the base model determines the performance of the agent, and the scaffold modulates the interaction only marginally.

sections.title: Epistemological structure of agent reasoning
sections.content: To examine how agents reason, we analyzed the full agent traces. We classified each step in an agents trajectory according to the epistemic operation it performs: forming hypothesis (H), gathering evidence (E), testing prediction (T), justifying conclusion (J), updating belief (U), or committing to hypothesis (C). We then represented the dependencies among these operations as directed graph in which each node is an epistemic operation and each edge dependency or reference between operations (Figure 4A). We observe that different reasoning modes produce different structural signatures. Popperian cycle of conjecture and refutation generates loops between H, T, and nodes. Abductive inference produces converging structures where multiple nodes feed into H. linear chain of tool calls with no back-references produces path graph with minimal branching (see Methods Section 4.8 for more details and Section H.1 for the full annotation protocol). Reasoning breakdowns predominate. We compiled catalog of productive reasoning patterns and anti-patterns, defined as structural templates over the annotated graphs (see Table H.14 and Table H.15 for definitions and illustration of graphs), and measured their prevalence across traces (Figure 4B). Untested claims, hypotheses stated without experiments designed to test them, appear in 53% of traces overall and 63% in hypothesis-driven domains. Evidence non-uptake, gathering result and proceeding without incorporating it, occurs in 68% of traces. In 71% of traces, beliefs are never updated. Only 26% of traces exhibit refutation-driven belief revision. Convergent multi-test evidence, multiple independent lines of evidence brought to bear on single hypothesis, occurs rarely (7%). Evidence-led hypothesis generation occurs in 65% of traces but coexists with the high rates of evidence non-uptake and untested claims. While abductive reasoning occurs, downstream testing and revision, in most traces, do not. Representative trace excerpts for each breakdown category are shown in Extended Data Figure 4; the annotated traces are browsable at https://lamalab-org.github.io/corral/#explainers (see Section H.7). Reasoning does not adapt to epistemic demand. Agents do not adapt their reasoning to the task, as human practitioner would. Ion identification rewards hypothesis-test cycles, retrosynthesis rewards combinatorial tree search, and molecular simulation rewards simulate-validate-refine loop; yet the topology of agent reasoning is the same across all three. Evidence non-uptake rates are 82% in workflow domains, 66% in strategic reasoning, and 60% in hypothesis-driven domains (Figure 4C). Convergent multi-test evidence remains at or below 10% in every domain group. Increasing problem scope within domain produces no corresponding shift in the agents epistemic operations (Figure H.18). Agents apply single reasoning mode across the full epistemic range. This mode is adequate for workflow execution but not for the disciplined treatment of evidence that hypothesis-driven inquiry requires (Figure 2). 8 Figure 4: Reasoning breakdowns dominate across all domain groups. (A) Epistemological annotation process. Agent traces are annotated in two stages using the model Claude Sonnet 4.5 : first, each step is labeled with an epistemic operation (hypothesis, test, evidence, judgment, update, commitment); second, directed edges capture dependencies between operations. The annotation process is described in detail in Section H.1. (B) Frequency of each pattern across domain groups. Bar plots show that reasoning-breakdown rates exceed productive-motif rates in every domain group; detailed scores are in Table H.16. This invariance extends to the model level. Models differ in the raw frequency of individual operations, with stronger models producing more and nodes per trace, but the topology of their reasoning graphs is similar. Higher-performing models retrieve more relevant knowledge and execute procedures more accurately. The epistemic structure of their reasoning does not change correspondingly.

sections.title: Trace interventions and reliability
sections.content: The reasoning breakdowns documented above raise the question of whether they can be mitigated by providing agents with better context. To test this, we designed trace intervention experiment that provides an extreme form of scaffolding: injecting partial reasoning trajectories from prior runs into the agents conversation history before it begins new trial (methodology in Section 4.9). These interventions inject actual sequences of tool calls, observations, and reasoning steps: the content successful agent would have produced on its own. We varied both the source (successful or failed prior runs) and the extent (early steps only, or near-complete trajectories) of the injected traces (Figure 5A). The effectiveness of these interventions tracks epistemic demand. We compare each intervention to no-injection baseline (Section 4.9) and relate the results to token-level GPT-OSS-120B log-probabilities (Section 4.10). In workflow construction domains such as ML-based Property Prediction, where models exhibit high token-level confidence (mean log-probability 0.2 across the group; per-environment values in Figure G.14, Figure 5C), injecting one or two successful steps suffices to exceed baseline performance. Failed-trace interventions have little effect: success rates remain near baseline. In Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation, Inorganic Qualitative Analysis, and Retrosynthetic Planning, where token-level confidence is lower (mean log-probability 0.27), partial interventions do not improve performance. Gains emerge only when agents receive near-complete successful trajectories (steps n2 or n1). This has direct consequence for reliability. Passk, the probability that all independent trials succeed, decays rapidly in hypothesis-driven domains (Figure 5B). Under baseline conditions, Passk drops below 0.05 by = 46 for Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation and Inorganic Qualitative Analysis. Even under success-trace interventions at early steps, the decay is similar. Repeated attempts do not yield consistent success. This pattern holds across both scaffold architectures. ReAct and tool-calling agents exhibit similar dependence on late-stage successful steps and similar degradation under failed-trace interventions (Extended Data Figure 3). Across all four analyses (performance decomposition, latent factor modeling, epistemological trace analysis, and trace interventions), the same conclusion holds: the base model determines what LLM-based agents can do in scientific settings, and current base models do not exhibit the epistemic patterns characteristic of scientific reasoning. 10 Figure 5: Scaffold interventions rescue workflow execution but not hypothesisdriven reasoning; failures in the latter recur across repeated trials. (A) Success rates under trace interventions. Agents receive partial trajectory from prior successful run (success trace; blue) or prior failed run (failed trace; red) before attempting new trial. In workflow-construction environments (mean log-probability 0.20), one or two injected successful steps suffice to exceed baseline. In hypothesis-driven and strategicreasoning environments (Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation, Inorganic Qualitative Analysis, Retrosynthetic Planning; mean log-probability 0.27), gains emerge only at near-complete trajectories (steps n2 or n1). Shaded regions show 1 standard deviation. (B) Passk (all trials succeed) and Pass@k curves by environment group. In hypothesis-driven domains, Passk falls below 0.1 by = 4 under baseline conditions, and below 0.05 by = 46 even with success-trace interventions at early steps. (C) Mean token-level log-probability per domain group, ordered by epistemic demand. Domains where partial interventions fail show the most negative log-probabilities, linking intervention unrecoverability to low token-level confidence.

sections.title: 3 Discussion
sections.content: The question of whether machines can conduct scientific inquiry has accompanied AI since DENDRAL built explicit rules for generating and eliminating hypotheses from spectral evidence in 1965.1 Our data provide specific answer for current LLM-based agents. In workflow-construction domains, agents approach ceiling performance. In hypothesis-driven domains, they do not adapt their reasoning to the demands of the problem: evidence is ignored in 68% of traces, refutation-driven belief revision occurs in only 26%, and convergent multi-test evidence is rare. These patterns persist across scaffold architectures, across domains, and under interventions that provide near-complete successful trajectories. They are properties of the base model. The epistemic process by which result is reached determines whether the resulting knowledge is justified.13,14,27 An agent that reaches correct answer while ignoring contradictory evidence and leaving hypotheses untested produces result whose reliability on new problems is unknown. Much current engineering effort focuses on scaffolding (prompting strategies, orchestration logic, tool interfaces), but the reasoning patterns we document persist across all scaffold conditions we tested, including extreme interventions. Addressing them will likely require changes to how base models are trained: slower process than scaffold improvement, and one currently not guided by epistemic criteria. The framework we introduce can supply such guidance: each environment provides reproducible task, tools, and scoring function over agent trajectories, sufficient to define training signals on the reasoning process itself. Human scientists operate within institutional structures that enforce epistemic norms: peer review, replication, reputational consequences. LLM-based agents are subject to no such constraints. Evidence that reliance on AI assistance can erode independent problemsolving34 compounds this gap. Characterizing the epistemic behavior of these systems requires explicit measurement. The evaluation framework we introduce does this across tasks and configurations. New environments, models, scaffolds, and metrics can be contributed, allowing the community to track how the epistemic behavior of these systems changes as models evolve. The methods and findings extend beyond scientific agents to any setting where the reasoning process determines the value of the result. The tools scientists use shape the science they produce.10,11 LLM-based agents are now among those tools. Our analysis shows that their epistemic behavior does not exhibit the patterns that characterize scientific reasoning. As long as they are evaluated only by the answers they produce, this difference will remain invisible, and it will shape the knowledge they help produce.

sections.title: 4.1 Formalism
sections.content: We adopt the language decision process (LDP) framework,29,30 formulation of partially observable Markov decision process in which observations and actions are represented in text. An LLM-based agent implements composite policy πoverall that factors into two components: (1) πoverall(at st) = (cid:0)πLLM(at st), πscaffold(at st)(cid:1), where πLLM is the base-model policy governing token-level choices and πscaffold is the scaffold policy governing prompting strategy, tool routing, memory management, and orchestration logic. The state st = (P, ht) comprises the task prompt and the conversation history ht at step t, which accumulates prior actions, tool outputs, and observations. At each step, the agent selects an action at (a tool call, reasoning step, or final answer) according to πoverall. The environment returns an observation, the history grows, and the cycle repeats. Tools are part of the environment, not the agent. Each environment defines set of callable functions with standardized docstrings; the agent receives no information beyond what the tool descriptions and returned observations provide. This separation is mirrored directly in our code implementation (Section C): the environment serves tools, task descriptions, and scoring functions through common interface, while the agent interacts solely through text-based requests. Because all agent configurations face identical tool interfaces and environment dynamics, observed differences in performance and behavior can be attributed to the base model or the scaffold.

sections.title: 4.2 Task and tool formalism
sections.content: Tasks and subtasks. benchmark item is either single task or task group = {τ1, . . . , τK} whose dependencies form directed acyclic graph: each subtask consumes the outputs of its predecessors. The framework resolves this graph and presents subtasks to the agent sequentially, injecting predecessor outputs into the prompt at each step. An ML-based Property Prediction task, for example, decomposes into data retrieval, feature engineering, model training, and evaluation, with each subtask consuming the artifacts produced by the previous one. Task prompt. The message sequence presented to the base model comprises system prompt, an environment-specific task prompt (goal description, submission format, and any input data from predecessor subtasks), scaffold-specific user template that adds responseformat instructions, and tool descriptions filtered by the verbosity axis defined below (see Section 4.2). Only the task prompt varies across environments; all other components are held constant. Full construction details are given in Section C.1. 13 Tool verbosity. Tool docstrings are authored with tagged sections (such as [BRIEF], [DETAILED], [EXAMPLES]; full tag definitions in Section C.2). Three levels expose cumulative subsets to the agent: brief includes only the [BRIEF] tag; workflow extends through [WORKFLOW_INTEGRATION]; comprehensive adds examples, error conditions, and limitations. Tool descriptions and task prompts are constructed independently, so verbosity varies without altering the task, enabling controlled ablation over the information available to πLLM.

sections.title: 4.3 Agent implementations
sections.content: All experiments use Corral, the evaluation framework we developed for this study (Section C). Only the model backend and agent scaffold vary across runs. We evaluated three models: GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06) via the OpenAI API, Claude Sonnet 4.5 (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929) via the Anthropic API, and GPT-OSS-120B via the Blablador API. Temperature was set to 0.0 for all runs, and each environment enforces fixed iteration limit. Tool verbosity was set to brief for main-text results; workflow and comprehensive settings appear in the appendix. We used two agent scaffolds. The first is ReAct:31 the agent produces reasoning traces and tool calls in single text stream. The second is structured tool calling: the API parses tool names and arguments from the model output, the agent executes the call, appends the result to the conversation history, and repeats until termination criterion is met. The two scaffolds differ in how actions are expressed, but the surrounding benchmark logic is identical. Scope and measurement caveats, including compute budget and malformedresponse rates, are detailed in Section I.

sections.title: 4.4 Domains and environments
sections.content: Each domain is realized as one or more environments providing tools, task description, and scoring function. Observations combine structured data (numerical values, tables, spectra) with natural-language summaries at the selected verbosity level. Scoring functions evaluate both the final answer and, where applicable, intermediate subtask completions. Each environment is backed by domain-specific engine, instrument, or dataset, ranging from LAMMPS simulations and live atomic force microscope to custom thermodynamic equilibrium engine and manually curated spectra (Table D.3). The inputs each environment receives and the artifacts each produces are shown in Extended Data Figure 1. Below, we summarize each domain. Full tool lists, scoring rubrics, and task specifications for the Inorganic Qualitative Analysis environment appear in Section D.1; details for every environment are at https://lamalab-org.github.io/corral/#environments. Spectroscopic structure elucidation. The agent determines the molecular structure of an unknown compound by requesting and interpreting spectroscopic data.3537 Available 14 tools include mass spectrometry, 1H and 13C NMR, HSQC, and infrared spectroscopy, alongside reference databases for chemical shift ranges, isotope distributions, and degree-ofunsaturation calculations. The agent can validate proposed structures and simulate spectra for comparison. Each task comprises 10 sequential subtasks that progress from molecular formula determination through functional group identification to complete SMILES assignment. Two scopes vary the molecular complexity: the first isolates individual analytical steps; the second requires integrating ambiguous, multi-modal evidence. HSQC acquisition is marked as expensive, introducing cost-information tradeoff. Inorganic qualitative analysis. In custom-built simulation environment, the agent identifies unknown cations in solution using the systematic wet-laboratory procedure taught in introductory chemistry. Tools include adding reagents, performing flame tests, measuring pH, centrifuging, decanting, heating, and observing color and precipitate changes. The environment simulates real chemical equilibria; observations are computed from thermodynamic data (rather than scripted). Three scopes increase the number of candidate ions from 35 common species with distinct chemistry to 15 or more, including ions with overlapping reactivity (e.g. Ag+, Hg2+), progressively requiring the agent to design discriminating experimental sequences rather than follow decision tree. Sample volume is limited, penalizing redundant experiments. Circuit inference. The agent recovers the topology and component values of hidden resistor network from pairwise resistance measurements between nodes. Tools provide series and parallel resistance calculations, delta-wye and wye-delta transforms, resistance measurement between specified node pairs, and circuit validation. Six tasks progress from simple parallel-series networks (67 resistors) through linear chains (10 resistors) to complex topologies (13+ resistors with multiple interaction points). This domain isolates logical and mathematical reasoning from domain-specific scientific knowledge. Retrosynthetic planning. The agent designs multi-step synthetic routes to target molecule under cost, step-count, and commercial-availability constraints. Tools include template catalog searchable by functional group or bond type, template application and step verification, commercial-availability lookup with CAS number conversion, and functionaland protecting-group detection. Three scopes increase target complexity from simple molecules with one-step solutions to complex natural products requiring up to five steps within $9,999 budget, where all leaf nodes must be commercially available. AFM experiment execution. The agent operates an atomic force microscope, executes the experimental procedure, and analyzes the resulting nanoscale surface characterization data. The four scopes vary in the degree of guidance: the first specifies all scan parameters for identical measurements, while the last provides only the objective and expects the 15 agent to choose scan areas and derive mathematical relationship. The Image Optimizer tool is removed at higher scopes, introducing tradeoff between available assistance and independent reasoning about scan modes and parameters. Molecular simulation. The agent designs and executes molecular dynamics simulations using the LAMMPS package38 to predict materials properties. Tools cover the full workflow: retrieving crystal structures from the Materials Project,39,40 converting to LAMMPS data format, querying force-field metadata, running simulations, and analyzing output logs. Two scopes vary the degree of procedural guidance: at the first, the simulation protocol is prescribed; at the second, the agent must select simulation parameters, design the heating or cooling schedule, and choose appropriate analysis methods. Tasks span quenching, surface energy computation, and melting simulations, with quantitative targets and specified tolerances (e.g. self-diffusivity of molten SiO2 within 20%). Adsorption surface construction. The agent builds an adsorbate-slab configuration from bulk crystal structures and molecules for heterogeneous catalysis studies. Tools integrate with the Materials Project to retrieve structures and polymorph data, generate surface slabs for specified Miller indices, vacuum, and enumerate adsorption sites. The agent must select an appropriate bulk polymorph, generate surface termination, identify relevant adsorption configurations, and place the adsorbate molecule on the adsorption site. ML-based property prediction. The agent assembles complete machine-learning pipeline to predict formation energies of material polymorphs. Tools cover data retrieval from the Materials Project (batch and single-composition queries), dataset filtering and consolidation, tabular feature engineering, XGBoost41 training with configurable hyperparameters, evaluation, and cross-validation. Tasks span three material classes (oxides, nitrides, sulfides). Models are scored against specified R2 thresholds and cross-validation targets.

sections.title: 4.5 Manual trace annotation
sections.content: We built custom web application to support the manual annotation described in Figure F.4 (see the annotation app at https://lamalab-org.github.io/corral/#annotate). The tool displays reconstructed agent traces as interactive graphs. Each node represents single message-like event, such as user instructions, reasoning steps, or tool calls. Annotators process traces in order. They label only behavior-relevant nodes; by design, system messages, tool outputs, and the initial task description are not annotatable. For each eligible node, annotators select one or more markers from taxonomy we developed for this study. The taxonomy distinguishes positive behaviors (e.g., planning statements, validation attempts), failure modes (e.g., hallucinations, unnecessary tool use), and neutral states (detailed in Section F). Annotators can also add free-text observations to each node and trace. The 16 interface requires left-to-right progression and checks that all annotatable nodes receive at least one marker before submission. For downstream analysis, two expert annotators completed the full annotation process on 773 selected traces. Each selected trace received annotations from one domain expert. The two annotators first annotated and discussed several calibration traces and edge cases to align their understanding. This process produced one final annotation record per trace, which informed all later marker-level analyses.

sections.title: 4.6 Diagnostic question-answer pairs
sections.content: For each domain, we designed two types of diagnostic questions. Knowledge questions target the specific facts needed to complete tasks; reasoning questions target the specific inference operations the environment requires. The designer of each environment compiled the initial set; submissions were made via pull requests and accepted only after two independent expert reviews. Knowledge question counts range from approximately twenty to over one hundred per environment; reasoning question counts are more uniform, with twenty to twenty-five per environment. All three models were evaluated on the full item bank at temperature of 0.0. Full results are detailed in Table E.6; the item format, scoring rubric, and review protocol are described in Section E.

sections.title: 4.7 IRT and phenomenological model
sections.content: We adopt two-stage modeling strategy to (i) estimate the latent cognitive capabilities that each model brings to each scientific domain, and (ii) quantify how those capabilities, together with agent-design choices, predict benchmark success as illustrated in Figure 3 A. Extracting model capabilities. two-parameter logistic (2PL) IRT model estimates latent abilities from binary QA responses. The diagnostic questions are described in Section 4.6. For model answering item i: (Yij = 1) = σ(cid:0)ai (θj bi)(cid:1), (2) where θj is the latent ability of model j, ai > 0 is item discrimination, and bi is item difficulty. We fit two separate 2PL models, one to knowledge items and one to reasoning items, yielding θ(K) and θ(R) per model-environment pair. Both are standardized to zero mean and unit variance before entering Stage 2. j Phenomenological latent factor models. The IRT-derived abilities serve as covariates in second modeling stage that predicts agent success on the full benchmark. Raw trial outcomes are first balanced (equal task counts per environment) and aggregated into Binomial counts (ksuccess, ntrials) per unique combination of model, environment, scaffold, scope, tool-description verbosity, aggregation grain (task-level vs. subtask-level outcome), 17 and task identifier (the specific task-scope combination). The standardized IRT abilities θK and θR are then merged as continuous predictors. We specify family of Bayesian generalised linear models42 (M1M8; Section G.5.1) of increasing structural complexity, all sharing the Binomial likelihood Binomial(n, p), logit(p) = η, (3) and common set of sum-to-zero categorical effects for scaffold (γ), verbosity (ξ), and scope (κ). Models differ in how they partition the remaining variance. Candidate specifications are compared using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling leave-one-out cross-validation (PSIS-LOO; Figure G.9). The best-fitting model is M7, which allows knowledge and reasoning slopes to vary by environment scope. All main-text variance decompositions and predicted success probabilities are derived from M7 (see Section G.5.1).

sections.title: 4.8 Epistemological graphs
sections.content: We sampled traces balanced across environments, models, and problem scope levels, prioritizing task diversity. Only ReAct traces were used, as they contain explicit reasoning steps. Tool description verbosity was set to brief; differences between verbosity levels are minimal (see Figure G.15). Each trace was annotated using Claude 4.5 Sonnet in two stages. In the first stage, the model labels nodes from short, overlapping windows of messages. Node labels correspond to epistemic operations: hypothesis, test, evidence, judgment, update, and commitment. In the second stage, the model adds directed edges between related nodes. Edge types include testing, observing, using, contradicting, competing, and updating. In both stages, the temperature was set to 0.7. Every node and edge includes supporting quote and the index of the source message, grounding annotations in the trace. After processing the full trace, repeated items are normalized and duplicates removed. Section H.1 details the annotation process. Annotations were validated in two ways. Automated checks verify that each supporting quote appears in the cited message and that each edge connects valid nodes. Expert review guided the annotation design: an expert iteratively revised prompts, labels, and markers until annotations matched their own interpretation of the traces. Inter-annotator agreement analysis on representative sample of 25 traces showed substantial humanhuman agreement (overall 92.6%, mean PABAK 0.853) and even higher humanLLM agreement (95.7%), confirming that the automated pipeline performs within the range of human expert judgment. Section H.5 provides deeper analysis of the annotation results. Productive patterns and anti-patterns are defined as explicit structural templates over the annotated graphs (Table H.14, Table H.15). Productive patterns include Popperian falsification,43 exploratory-to-confirmatory transitions,44 and active learning.45,46 Antipatterns are drawn from established epistemic failures in scientific reasoning and from 18 behaviors observed in the traces, including untested hypotheses, ignored evidence, and unresolved contradictions. Both are grouped into higher-level families for aggregated analysis (Table H.16).

sections.title: 4.9 Trace intervention experiment
sections.content: The trace intervention experiment probes whether agent performance is driven by latent model capability or by accumulated conversational context. We first ran 15 trials per task across all environments at temperature 0.7 to populate registry of successful and failed execution traces. moderate temperature follows standard practice for balancing sample diversity against coherence.47 These runs are distinct from the main evaluation, which uses temperature 0.0 for deterministic scores. For each new trial, we inject partial trajectory from this registry into the agents conversation history before the trial begins. The injected traces are replayed, tool calls and environment interactions included, so that the agent resumes from consistent intermediate state. We vary two factors: the source of the injected trace (successful or failed prior run) and its extent (early steps 12 versus near-complete trajectories at steps n2 or n1). The intervention supplies the actual reasoning and actions successful agent would have produced rather than instructions or prompting strategies. Intervention trials also use temperature 0.7. Protocol details and pseudocode are in Section G.6.

sections.title: 4.10 Token-level log-probability analysis
sections.content: To quantify the models token-level confidence across benchmark environments, we analyze the log-probabilities returned by the language model for each generated token. For every assistant message produced during trial, we select and save the log-probability of the top-1 token at each decoding step. Formally, for response consisting of tokens {w1, w2, . . . , wT }, ℓt = log pθ(wt w<t, x), = 1, . . . , T, (4) where pθ denotes the language model, w<t is the preceding token sequence, and is the input context (which includes system prompt, task prompt, tool definitions, and conversation history). For each environment e, we pool all non-special token log-probabilities across every assistant message from every trial in that environment. Tokens whose log-probability is exactly zero and that correspond to special control tokens (e.g., <endoftext>, <im_end>) are excluded. The per-environment mean log-probability is then, ℓe =

More negative values of ℓe signal that the model distributes probability more diffusely, reflecting greater uncertainty. Per-environment values and interpretation are reported in Section G.7.

sections.title: A Related work
sections.content: . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 88 . 90 . 92 . 98 103 Corral sits at the intersection of five lines of research: benchmarks for scientific agents, endto-end AI scientist systems, the attribution of agent capability to the base model versus the scaffold, psychometric measurement of model ability, and process-level epistemic evaluation of AI-generated science. A.1 Benchmarks for scientific agents Existing benchmarks for scientific agents primarily score outcomes. Domain-specific evaluations such as ChemBench, MaCBench, and Lab-Bench probe knowledge and reasoning in chemistry, materials science, and biology through curated question sets.13 Workfloworiented benchmarks including ScienceAgentBench, SciAgentGym, and Curie evaluate endto-end execution of data analyses and discovery pipelines drawn from published work.46 Replication-oriented evaluations such as Lessons-Learned, MLR-Bench, ResearchBench, and DeepResearch test whether agents can reproduce or extend specific results.710 AstaBench and BixBench evaluate agents across the full research pipeline, including manuscript-style outputs.11,12 Generalist agent benchmarks such as AgentBench, GAIA, Gaia2, MultiAgentBench, and ToolSandbox1317 measure capabilities that scientific agents draw on but were not designed for science. These evaluations score what an agent produced, not how it produced it. correct answer reached by lookup is scored identically to the same answer reached by hypothesis testing; an incorrect answer reached by careful inquiry is scored identically to one reached by confabulation. Concerns that benchmarks measure narrow skill rather than the underlying capability they purport to assess are long-standing.18 Corral preserves the outcome metrics these benchmarks provide and adds standardized environments with graded task scopes and fine-grained subtask diagnostics. A.2 AI scientists growing body of work develops LLM-based systems intended to conduct end-to-end research: hypothesis generation, experimental design, execution, and write-up. Sakanas AI Scientist19 produces submission-ready manuscripts; Kosmos20 reports cross-domain discoveries; ChemCrow,21 Coscientist,22 the El Agente series,23 Organa,24 and AlphaEvolve25 target 31 specific scientific or engineering domains. Multi-agent architectures such as SciAgents, ScienceClaw, and virtual laboratories structure the workflow with role specialization or knowledge-graph guidance,2628 building on earlier instantiations for protein design29 and finite-element mechanics;30 recent work also adapts the objective each sub-agent optimizes to mitigate reward hacking.31 Complementary efforts target reasoning at the base-model level, training models to produce reflection and recursive refinement steps explicitly.32 Alongside this systems-building effort, risk-focused perspectives argue that AI scientists introduce novel vulnerabilities requiring explicit safeguards across user intent, agent alignment, and environmental feedback.33 These systems are deployed and reported on without shared instrument for assessing the quality of the reasoning that produced their outputs. Corral is designed to fill this role: its environments, tools, scoring functions, and runner can be reused as building blocks for new agents and scaffolds, and the diagnostic and epistemic instruments introduced here can be applied to any system built on top. The environments can also serve as training substrates: each supplies reproducible tasks and scoring over agent trajectories, sufficient to optimize agents against criteria defined on the reasoning process.34 A.3 Scaffolds and the base-model/scaffold attribution The capability of an LLM-based agent reflects two contributions: the base models tokenlevel competence and the scaffolds orchestration logic. Prompt-based scaffolds such as Chain-of-Thought, ReAct, and self-consistency,3537 and agentic scaffolds with tool access such as Toolformer, Voyager, and Reflexion,3840 are now standard, and gains over base models are routinely reported. The question of how much of any gain is the scaffold and how much is the base model is rarely answered directly. Meireles et al.41 are among the few to address it, showing that effective scaffolding preserves coordination scaling laws that deteriorate without it. Corral varies model and scaffold orthogonally on fixed task set, allowing each contribution to be read directly from the results. A.4 Item response theory for model evaluation Item response theory (IRT), developed for educational assessment,4244 models benchmark performance as the interaction between latent ability and item characteristics such as difficulty and discrimination. Early NLP applications used IRT to construct evaluation datasets and demonstrated that item difficulty predicts model behavior more reliably than aggregate accuracy.45,46 Bayesian IRT-based leaderboards introduced uncertainty-aware ability estimates,47 and subsequent work showed that hidden implementation choices distort raw rankings in ways that IRT can correct.48,49 For LLM agents, IRT-inspired task selection has been used to preserve ranking fidelity across scaffolds and environments.50 We apply IRT to our diagnostic subtask items to estimate latent capability profiles per 32 modelenvironment pair, separating ability from item difficulty in way that aggregate Pass@k does not. A.5 Epistemic evaluation of AI in science Process-level epistemic evaluation of AI-generated science remains underdeveloped. Fieldlevel critiques are well established: Ioannidis et al.51 on irreproducibility in the empirical sciences, and Hao et al.52 on machine learning, narrowing the hypothesis space scientists pursue. More directly on large language models, Mitchell & Krakauer53 survey the debate over whether such systems exhibit genuine understanding, noting that surface-level competence on benchmarks does not settle the underlying epistemic question. Thais et al.54 extend these concerns to LLM-based scientific systems specifically, arguing that current AIfor-science work conflates predictive accuracy with epistemic warrant and lacks operational instruments for assessing whether claims are justified in the philosophical sense.55 Corral provides such an instrument. The behavioral analysis introduced here annotates reasoning traces as graphs of operations and detects structural patterns of productive inquiry, such as Popperian falsification, exploratory-to-confirmatory transitions, and active learning,5659 as well as patterns of epistemic failure, such as untested hypotheses, ignored evidence, and unresolved contradictions. This shifts the evaluation from whether an agent reached the right answer to the process by which it arrived there. A.6 Rise of AI Scientists Following Hao et al.52, we quantified the emergence of AI-scientist research within chemistry and materials science and contextualized its growth against the broader AI/ML-forchemistry-and-materials literature. We constructed narrow corpus targeting AI scientists, LLM agents, autonomous discovery systems, and closely related agentic workflows. Using OpenAlex,60 we first retrieved the broader set of AIand ML-related papers in chemistry and materials science, then applied stricter keyword-based filter to isolate publications matching our AI-scientist definition. Figure A.1 reports yearly publication counts for both corpora on log scale, along with the share of AI-scientist papers within the broader AI-for-chemistry literature. 33 Figure A.1: Sharp rise of AI-scientist publications within chemistry and materials AI literature. Annual publication counts (log scale) for the broader AI-for-chemistryand-materials corpus versus strict AI-scientist subset. The right axis shows the share of AI-scientist papers within the broader corpus, revealing steep recent increase. Two patterns are immediately apparent. First, the broader AI-for-chemistry literature has reached roughly 104 papers per year, while the strict AI-scientist subset has grown from about 101 papers in 2018 to the upper 102 range in the most recent partial yearalready exceeding earlier full-year totals. Thus, although AI-scientist work remains much smaller than AI for chemistry as whole, it is no longer marginal subfield but recognizable and rapidly growing research stream. Second, the relative share of AI-scientist papers within the AI-for-chemistry literature is rising even faster than absolute counts. This strict subset accounted for only about 1% of AI-for-chemistry output through 2024, increased to roughly 2% in 2025, and approaches 5% in the current partial year. Consequently, while AI-scientist research still represents very small fraction of all chemistry and materials science publishingapproximately 0.01% through 2024 and around 0.06% in 2025its footprint within the AI segment of the field is expanding rapidly.

sections.title: B Glossary
sections.content: Domain. One of the eight scientific areas covered by the benchmark (e.g., retrosynthetic planning, atomic force microscopy); see Section 4.4. Scope. difficulty or ablation variant within domain, varying task complexity, tool availability, or degree of agent guidance. Scopes are introduced per domain in Section 4.4. Environment. concrete instance of domain at specific scope, formalized as language decision process in Section 4.1. Each environment exposes tools, task description, and scoring function through the Corral HTTP interface. Task. single problem an agent is asked to solve within an environment; may decompose into dependency-ordered subtasks (Section 4.1). Subtask. component of task whose output is forwarded as input to downstream subtasks in the dependency graph (Section 4.1). Scaffold. The orchestration layer wraps base LLM: prompt formatting, tool routing, history management, and termination logic. The two scaffolds evaluated here are ReAct and structured tool calling (Section 4.3). Tool-documentation verbosity. The level of detail retained in tool docstrings presented to the agent varied as an experimental axis (Section 4.2). Configuration. particular combination of base model, scaffold, tool-documentation verbosity, and environment. Trial. single end-to-end execution of an agent configuration on task. Trace. The conversation history produced during trial is used downstream for epistemological annotation (Section 4.8). Pass@k. Probability that at least one of independent trials on task succeeds. Estimated (cid:1), where is the number of from trials by the unbiased estimator 1 (cid:0)nc successful trials. (cid:1)/(cid:0)n Passk. Probability that all independent trials on task succeed. reliability measure, unlike Pass@k, requires every trial to succeed rather than at least one. Language decision process (LDP). The formal model of agentenvironment interaction adopted here: partially observable Markov decision process whose observations and actions are represented in text (Section 4.1). ELPD-LOO. Expected log predictive density estimated by leave-one-out cross-validation; used to compare the candidate hierarchical models in Section G.5. 35 Inter-annotator agreement (κ). Cohens κ between two independent annotators on the same set of traces, reported in Section H, to quantify the reliability of the manual review. Epistemic operation. node type in the annotated reasoning graph, drawn from the fixed vocabulary {hypothesis, test, evidence, judgment, update, commitment}. Each operation corresponds to an identifiable epistemic act in the trace (Section 4.8). Item response theory (IRT). psychometric modeling framework that jointly estimates respondent ability and item difficulty from matrix of correct/incorrect outcomes. We fit the two-parameter logistic (2PL) form, in which each item has difficulty and discrimination a, and each respondent latent ability θ (Section G.4). Latent ability (θ). The IRT parameter representing models capability on set of items, separated from the items own difficulty. Higher θ corresponds to higher probability of success on items of given difficulty. Posterior predictive check (PPC). fitted Bayesian models predictions for held-out observations are compared against the observed outcomes to assess calibration and generalization. Reported for the selected hierarchical model in Section G.5. Capability item. questionanswer item used to estimate latent model capability through IRT (Section 4.6, Section E). Productive motif. graph template over the annotated trace that matches recognized practice of disciplined inquiry: Popperian falsification, exploratory-to-confirmatory transition, active-learning-style test redesign (Section H.3). Reasoning breakdown. graph template over the annotated trace that matches an established epistemic failure mode: untested claim, evidence non-uptake, fixed belief trace, contradiction without repair (Section H.3).

sections.title: C Corral
sections.content: : Framework Corral is composable evaluation framework that separates environments from agents through standard HTTP interface. Environments are hosted by FastAPI server that exposes task descriptions, tools, and scoring through small set of RESTful endpoints (Table C.2, Figure C.2). Agents interact with an environment solely by issuing HTTP requests. The Corral runner drives evaluation by iterating trials over registered agentenvironment pairs, checkpointing intermediate state, and aggregating results into per-configuration scores and diagnostics. The snippets below illustrate the three main extension points; the canonical API reference is maintained at https://lamalab-org.github.io/corral. 36 Tools. Tools are formally subclasses of the Tool base class. The @tool decorator is convenience shortcut that wraps plain Python function into such subclass. In both cases, the docstring of the tool is part of the contract between environment and agent: Corral exposes it to the agent as the tools natural-language description, and its tagged sections are filtered by the tool-documentation verbosity axis (Section 4.2). Listing 1: Defining tool via the decorator shortcut. from corral . backend . tool import tool @tool def my_custom_tool ( input_param : str ) -> str : " " " [ BRIEF ] One - sentence summary . Args : input_param : description of the parameter . Returns : description of the return value . " " " return " Processed : { input_param } " 1 2 3 4 6 7 8 9 10 12 Environments. An environment subclasses Environment, attaches its tools in the constructor, and overrides get_task_prompt and score. dictionary of environment instances is then served over HTTP by create_benchmark_server. Listing 2: Defining and serving an environment. from corral . backend import Environment from corral . backend . server import cr e _b hm rk _ se rv import os , uvicorn BASE_WORK_DIR = os . getenv ( " CORRAL_WORK_DIR " ) class MyEnvironment ( Environment ) : def __init__ ( self , task_id , problem , answer , base_workdir = BASE_WORK_DIR ) : self . problem = problem self . correct_answer = answer super () . __init__ ( task_id , base_workdir ) self . add_tool ( my_custom_tool ) def get_task_prompt ( self ) : return " Solve this problem : { self . problem } " def score ( self ) : if self . state . submitted_answer is None : return 0.0 return 1.0 if self . state . submitted_answer == self . correct_answer else 0.0 1 3 4 5 6 7 9 10 11 12 13 15 16 17 18 19 21 37 22 23 24 26 27 28 29 30 environments = { " task_1 " : MyEnvironment ( " task_1 " , " Problem 1 " , " Answer 1 " ) , " task_2 " : MyEnvironment ( " task_2 " , " Problem 2 " , " Answer 2 " ) , } if __name__ == " __main__ " : app = cr e _ c hm rk _s e ( environments ) uvicorn . run ( app , host = " 0.0.0.0 " , port =8000) Agents. An agent subclasses BaseAgent and implements run. The agent drives inquiry through the router interface: get_task_guide returns the task prompt, execute_tool invokes tool, and get_llm_response routes message list through the configured base model. Listing 3: Defining custom scaffold. from corral . agents import BaseAgent from corral . agents . prompt_utils import create_prompt from corral . agents . utils import LiteLLMMessage class MyAgent ( BaseAgent ) : def __init__ ( self , model : str , ** kwargs ) : super () . __init__ ( model , ** kwargs ) def run ( self , interface , task_id : str ) -> str : task_guide = interface . get_task_guide ( task_id ) messages = create_prompt ( task_guide ) # loop : call base LLM , parse action , execute tool , # append observation , repeat until termination response = self . get_llm_response ( messages ) return response 2 3 4 5 6 8 9 10 11 12 14 15 Runner. The Corral runner iterates over tasks and trials, dispatches agent calls through the router, persists intermediate state for fault tolerance, and returns unified result summary. Experiment execution and environment definition are kept separate. 1 2 4 5 6 7 8 Listing 4: Runner invocation with custom diagnostic metric. from corral import CorralRouter , CorralRunner from corral . agents import MyAgent # Connect to the environment server router = CorralRouter () # Configure the agent ( model + scaffold ) 38 9 11 12 13 14 15 17 18 19 20 21 agent = MyAgent ( model = " gpt -4 " , temperature =0.7 , max_iterations =100) # Create runner and register custom metrics runner = CorralRunner ( router , agent ) # Run evaluation result = runner . bench ( [ " task_1 " , " task_2 " ] , trials_per_task =3 , verbose = True ) # Results include default + custom metrics print ( " Average score : { result . metrics [ ' average_score ']} " ) C.1 Prompt construction The message sequence delivered to the base model is assembled from four independently authored components. System prompt. static instruction, identical across all configurations, that establishes the agents role and context management directive. Task prompt. Each environment implements get_task_prompt() method that assembles structured prompt from the task definition (see the endpoints in Table C.2). All environments follow common template: (i) task name, (ii) natural-language description of the scientific goal, (iii) the required submission format (e.g., SMILES string, list of ions), and (iv) any available input data. For task groups, the input data include the artifacts forwarded from predecessor subtasks in the dependency graph, plus initial parameters such as target molecule or material composition. Some environments prepend brief role preamble or append workspace instructions when filesystem tools are available. Scaffold template. The task prompt is inserted into scaffold-specific user template. For ReAct, the template embeds the task prompt together with textual tool-usage guide and instructs the model to respond in XML-tagged thoughtactionobservation turns; the final answer is submitted via <final_answer> tag. For structured tool calling, the template contains only the task prompt; tool schemas are passed separately through the native function-calling API, and the final answer is submitted with Final Answer: keyword prefix. Tool descriptions. Filtered by the verbosity axis (Section 4.2) and injected either inline in the user prompt (ReAct) or via the function-calling API (tool calling). 39 Only the task prompt varies across environments; the system prompt, scaffold template, and tool-injection mechanism are held constant. C.2 Example of different verbosity The example boxes below render the docstring of the tool search_by_smiles at each of the three verbosity levels. Section 4.2 defines the basis on which information is controlled for different verbosity levels. Table C.1 lists the tagged sections and their content. Table C.1: Tagged sections used in tool docstrings. Each tools documentation is authored with the sections below. At each verbosity level, cumulative subset of these sections is retained (Section 4.2). sections.title: D Domains and environments
sections.content: Each of the eight domains described in Methods exposes multiple task scopes; each scope is realized as separate environment. The eight domains are summarised in Extended Data Figure 1, which shows the input the agent receives and the artifact it produces in each case. An overview of the characteristics of each domain is provided in Table D.3. Full per-scope tool lists, scoring rubrics, and task specifications are maintained at https://lamalaborg.github.io/corral/#environments. One environment, the inorganic qualitative analysis (wetlab), is used as worked example below (Section D.1). Table D.3: Overview of the Corral environments.For each of the eight scientific domains, the table reports: (i) the number of difficulty levels, (ii) the number of distinct tasks per level, (iii) the total number of domain-specific tools available to the agent, (iv) the typical trace length, and (v) the backing: the engine, instrument, dataset, or library on which the environment is built. The trace length is defined as the mean number of agent-generated messages (excluding system and initial user prompts), averaged across all models, agent architectures, and verbosity settings. Domain AFM experiment execution Adsorption surface construction Molecular simulation ML-based property prediction Circuit inference Retrosynthetic planning Spectroscopic structure elucidation Inorganic qualitative analysis Scopes Tasks per scope Tools Trace length Backing 1 2 1 1 3 2 1 3 2-3 3 6 8 10 6 26.3 15 8 9 15 19.6 30.4 16.6 15.0 25.5 15.1 14 39.4 Live atomic force microscope in the loop (DriveAFM (Nanosurf)61) Surface generation via pymatgen LAMMPS simulation engine Materials Project API dataset Custom circuit generation engine Novel tool suite built on an in-house reaction-template library assembled from scratch63 Manually curated spectral dataset6466 Thermodynamic equilibrium engine67 coupled to custom color-mixing engine D.1 Inorganic Qualitative Analysis Environment Example Figure D.3 overviews the agent workflow in this environment. 47 Figure D.3: Overview of the Inorganic Qualitative Analysis environment. The agents workflow consists of three main phases: (1) Initialization: The prompt is constructed with the task description, list of available tools (including their arguments), and the required response format. (2) Iterative Interaction: The agent calls tools to perform experiments (e.g., pH measurement, flame tests; detailed view of the tools is provided in Table H.19) and can reference external information, such as lists of possible ions, reagent properties, color indicators, and the current inventory. (3) Final Scoring: The agent outputs list of ions present in the sample. The environment scores this answer by comparing it to the ground truth, returning binary score based on its correctness.

sections.title: E Capability assessment items
sections.content: The latent capability profiles in Section G.4 are estimated from bank of question-answer items curated for each environment. For every environment, items are partitioned into knowledge set that probes domain-specific facts required to operate the environment and reasoning set that requires multi-step inference over domain concepts. Table E.4 and 48 Table E.5 show one representative item of each type per environment. Per-model scores on the full bank are in Table E.6. E.1 Item generation protocol Benchmark items follow structured review protocol. Items are authored by the domain expert responsible for the corresponding environment. Two categories of items are distinguished: knowledge items, which test factual and procedural understanding of the domain (e.g., instrument settings, simulation parameters), and reasoning items, which require multistep logical inference, scenario analysis, or quantitative problem-solving within the domain. Each collection of items is submitted as pull request. Two independent expert reviewers then assess the items against predefined rubric (see Section E.1). Items that do not pass receive change requests and return to the review stage for reassessment. Once approved, items undergo final review and are merged into the benchmark. Section E.1 illustrates at high level the protocol followed. After consolidation of the items, the dataset is pushed and maintained on Hugging Face68 for versioning. Item generation Quality assessment Acceptance Expert drafts QA items Knowledge + reasoning questions PR created Two expert reviewers Assess against rubric Changes requested Author revises items No Approved? Yes Final review + merge Items added to benchmark Benchmark updated Item format and scoring rubric Every benchmark item is fourto six-option multiplechoice question stored in JSON. Each item carries unique uuid, human-readable name, short description, and list of keywords whose first entry identifies the environment, 49 and whose second entry is either requires_knowledge or requires_reasoning. The question text and answer options are stored in an examples list, where each entry contains an input string (the question) and target_scores dictionary mapping every option to 1 (correct) or 0 (incorrect). Exactly one option is marked correct. All items use the metric multiple_choice_grade: model receives score of 1 if it selects all and only the correct option and 0 otherwise. canary string is prepended to every item to enable the detection of the benchmark contamination in training corpora. Listing Section E.1 shows an example item. Example format of benchmark item in JSON format. 1 2 3 4 6 7 8 9 10 12 13 14 15 16 18 19 20 21 { " name ": " AFM_QA_25 " , " uuid ": "5 f17549c -7 bf4 -4 eb6 - a547 -7274 c7dddcb3 " , " description ": " Reasoning - based " , " examples ": [ { " input ": " Which scanning mode best balances tip safety ?" , " target_scores ": { " Contact mode ": 0 , " Tapping mode ": 1 , " Lateral force mode ": 0 , " Conductive AFM " : 0 } } ] , " keywords ": [" requires_knowledge " , " AFM " , " microscopy " , " scanning "] , " metrics ": [" ulti ple_ choic e_gr ade "] , " preferred_score ": " ulti ple_ choi ce_gr ade " } Quality-assessment procedure Each pull request is reviewed by two independent expert reviewers who evaluate every item against the following rubric: 1. Correctness. The marked answer must be verifiably correct and all distractors genuinely incorrect; reviewers independently re-derive calculations where applicable. Items containing factual errors or misconceptions are rejected. Incorrect options must be plausible enough to require domain knowledge to eliminate. 50 2. Clarity and ambiguity. Questions must be unambiguous, use consistent notation (e.g. siunitx units, ce{} formulae, SMILES), expand domain-specific acronyms, and be compatible with the evaluation harness. 3. Originality. Questions must not be readily findable online or in standard textbooks; items that can be answered by retrieving verbatim source are rejected. 4. Scope and relevance. Each item must be tightly linked to its environments domain and must measure domain expertise rather than general instruction-following ability. Out-of-scope questions are rejected. 5. Difficulty and diversity. Items should pose genuine challenge to domain expert, and the item set. Each environment should cover broad range based on the tasks in the environment. 6. Format and metadata. Keyword ordering, the requires_knowledge/requires_reasoning tag, and the format of the JSON are verified for consistency. Table E.4: Example knowledge-based questions. For each environment, one representative factual question is shown, testing domain-specific knowledge required to operate the environment. The correct answer is indicated in the last column. Which of the following computational parameters or choices directly and fundamentally influence the accuracy of calculated molecular adsorption energies in periodic Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations? The density of the k-point mesh used for Brillouin zone integration. D. The total simulation time of molecular dynamics trajectory. E. This is task from an agent benchmark. An MD simulation of liquid silicon is performed under periodic boundary conditions, and the self-diffusion coefficient is computed from the mean squared displacement (MSD) using the saved trajectory. The observed MSD plateaus at long times and the computed diffusion coefficient is nearly zero, which is unphysical for liquid. Below are few scenarios that may contain the agents correct/incorrect reasoning expressed through script snippets. Scenario A: # Dump wrapped coordinates dump 1 all custom 100 traj.lammpstrj id type Scenario B: # Dump unwrapped coordinates dump 1 all custom 100 traj.lammpstrj id type xu yu zu Scenario C: # Dump scaled (fractional) wrapped coordinates dump 1 all custom 100 traj.lammpstrj id type xs ys zs Which scenario corresponds to valid reasoning that would fix the MSD and diffusion calculation? Table E.6 presents the scores for each model on knowledge and reasoning questions. Claude Sonnet 4.5 achieves the highest scores in most environments, winning or tying on 13 of 16 environmentquestion-type pairs. sections.title: F Marker manual annotation
sections.content: Domain experts annotated agent execution traces using custom web-based tool (see Figure F.4) that renders each trace as navigable sequence of steps: model outputs, tool 54 Figure F.4: Screenshot of the Agent Trace Visualizer annotation interface. The tool renders each execution trace as navigable sequence of steps (model outputs, tool calls, and environment responses), allowing the expert annotator to assign one or more behavioral markers from controlled taxonomy and optionally add free-text observations at the step level and an overall trace-level comment. calls, and environment responses. One expert was assigned to each scientific environment. Each expert reviewed all accepted traces within their assigned environment, covering both the GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 4.5 models, across combinations with ReAct and tool-calling agents for the different scopes. For each step in trace, the annotator assigned one or more behavioral markers drawn from controlled taxonomy (Table F.7). Annotators could supplement marker selections with free-text observations at the step level and an overall comment characterizing the trace as whole. As result, 773 traces were annotated end-to-end following this procedure. The detailed results of the marker counts are described in Section G.3. 55 Table F.7: Behavioral marker taxonomy used during expert annotation of agent execution traces. Markers are organized into three tiers: positive markers capture effective reasoning and task execution; neutral markers flag unremarkable steps; and negative markers identify failure modes ranging from hallucination and looping to premature answer submission. The agent recognizes dead end and pivots to an alternative approach. The agent articulates an explicit plan or subgoal before acting. The agent produces explicit reasoning connecting hypotheses to evidence. The agent submits final answer in the required format.
