sections.title: G Performance analysis
sections.content: The base model is the primary determinant of agent performance. Scaffold, tool-documentation verbosity, and item-level factors account for smaller share of the variance (Figure 2). Aggregate scores (Tables G.8 and G.9) and per-skill performance (Figure G.5) are examined in the following subsections. hierarchical Bayesian model of per-trial outcomes supplies the variance decomposition and task-level calibration check (Section G.5). two-parameter IRT analysis of curated question-answer items separates knowledge from reasoning ability for each modelenvironment pair (Section G.4). Intervention experiments on the conversation history (Section G.6) and ablations of tool-documentation verbosity (Section G.8) complement these analyses. G.1 Aggregate scores Table G.8 and Table G.9 present mean scores across all eight environments for each modelagent combination. Table G.8 reports task-level outcomes, where each cell is the mean over complete end-to-end trial episodes within the corresponding environmentscope bin. Table G.9 provides the analogous results at the subtask level; the assignments are divided over the individual constituent steps rather than over full trials, allowing fine-grained evaluation. Consequently, subtask scores are generally higher than task scores, because partial credit accumulates across trial even when the overall task is not solved. The ranking of models is largely consistent across both tables: Claude Sonnet 4.5 leads in most environments, followed by GPT-4o and GPT-OSS-120B , although the performance gap narrows for lower-complexity scopes (S1) and for environments that require tight multi-step planning (e.g., Retrosynthesis). ReAct and Tool-Calling agents perform comparably on average, with differences becoming more pronounced at higher scope levels and in workflow-construction environments (e.g., AFM Experiment, Molecular Simulation), where the structured action space of Tool Calling confers modest advantage. 57 Table G.8: Average scores by environment and scope for each model and agent type. R=ReAct, TC=Tool Calling. Environments are grouped by task category: Hypothesis-driven inquiry (Spectra Elucidation, Circuit Inference), Strategic reasoning (Retrosynthesis), and Workflow construction (AFM Experiment, Molecular Simulation, Adsorption Surface, ML Property). Scope levels (S1S4) reflect increasing task difficulty within each environment. Environment Scope Claude-4.5-Sonnet GPT-4o GPT-OSS-120B TC TC TC Hypothesis-driven inquiry Spectra Eluc. Inorg. Qual. Across most combinations, retrieval and execution scores are higher than reasoning and validation scores, and the spread across combinations is larger for the latter two categories. Reasoning and validation require logical inference, consistency checking, and hypothesis testing, whereas retrieval and execution test more routine information access and action selection. The pattern aligns with the gradient in epistemic demand reported in the main text. 59 Figure G.5: Performance decreases as epistemic demand increases. All tasks were broken down into subtasks, each classified by experts into four categories (retrieval, execution, reasoning, validation). Both scaffolds for all models show the same trend. G.3 Manual marker annotation Table G.10 presents the total occurrence counts of each behavioral marker across all annotated traces, broken down by model and scaffold. Positive markers are dominated by validation_attempt and reasoning_statement, with Claude Sonnet 4.5 producing substantially more explicit reasoning steps than GPT-4o across both scaffolds (e.g., 392 vs. 69 under ReAct). ReAct consistently elicits more planning_statement annotations for Claude Sonnet 4.5 (240 vs. 34 for tool calling), suggesting that the scaffold shapes highlevel planning behavior more for that model than for GPT-4o . Among negative markers, loop_instance and wrong_reasoning are the most prevalent across all conditions. GPT4o shows notably higher rates of non_sense and syntax_error under ReAct (141 and 100, respectively), pointing to formatting difficulties with that scaffold. misunderstood_tool is almost exclusively observed in the Claude Sonnet 4.5 tool-calling condition (31 out of 38 occurrences), indicating scaffold-specific failure mode. We estimate latent knowledge and reasoning capabilities for each model-environment pair using separate two-parameter logistic (2PL) item response theory (IRT) models.42 For each of the eight benchmark environments, we prepared two sets of question-answer (QA) items: one targeting domain-specific factual knowledge and one targeting multi-step reasoning (Table E.6; item generation and review protocol in Section E). All three models were evaluated on the full QA across all environments, yielding binary correctness outcomes. We fit one 2PL IRT model to the knowledge QA responses and second, independent 2PL IRT model to the reasoning QA responses. In each model, latent ability parameter θm,e is estimated for every modelenvironment pair 61 Figure G.6: Model capabilities vary sharply across scientific domains, with knowledge and reasoning deficits concentrated in the hypothesis-driven inquiry domain. Side-by-side heatmaps of the latent knowledge (θK, left) and reasoning (θR, right) capabilities estimated from two-parameter IRT model fit to task outcomes across all agent configurations. Rows correspond to the eight benchmark environments; columns show the three evaluated models. Cell values are posterior means of the per-model, per-environment capability parameters, with color intensity scaled from low (light) to high (dark purple), centered at zero. Both heatmaps share common y-axis for direct comparison. (m, e) via hierarchical prior θm,e (µm + νe, σθ), where µm and νe are the sum-to-zero model and environment effects. Item parameters follow the standard 2PL parameterization: discrimination ai = exp(log ai) with log ai (0, 0.5) and difficulty bi (0, 2), giving item response probability (correct) = logistic(aiθ bi). All parameters are estimated via MCMC (4 chains, 2000 post-warmup draws each, target acceptance 0.9); we report posterior means and standard deviations of θ. The knowledge model yields θK and the reasoning model yield θR, enabling direct comparison of where each models knowledge versus reasoning capabilities diverge across scientific domains. The extracted capabilities are plotted as heatmap in Figure G.6; complementary scatter view comparing knowledge against reasoning abilities per modelenvironment pair is shown in Figure G.7. 62 Figure G.7: Knowledge and reasoning capabilities are broadly correlated, but retrosynthesis demands reasoning far beyond what domain knowledge alone predicts. Scatter plot of latent knowledge capability (θK) against reasoning capability (θR) for each modelenvironment pair, estimated from two-parameter IRT model. Marker shape encodes the model (circle: Claude Sonnet 4.5 (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929); square: GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06); diamond: GPT-OSS (gpt-oss-120b)) and color encodes the domain group (blue: workflow construction; purple: hypothesis-driven inquiry; magenta: strategic reasoning). The dashed diagonal marks θK = θR; points above the line indicate environments where reasoning demands exceed knowledge demands. Retrosynthetic planning (magenta) sits far above the diagonal with strongly negative θK, suggesting models rely on chain-of-thought reasoning rather than retrievable domain knowledge to navigate the combinatorial search space. G.5 Binomial modeling The goal of the latent factor modeling stage is to quantify how much of the variance in agent success is attributable to model capability (knowledge and reasoning), how much to agentdesign choices (scaffold, verbosity). This decomposition is not uniquely determined since it depends on modeling assumptions about which factors interact and at what granularity grouping structure is imposed. For instance, task difficulty might be adequately captured at the environment level, or it might require finer environmentscope resolution. To avoid committing to single set of assumptions, we specified family of eight candidate models (M1M8) that span range of structural choices. All models share the same likelihood, priors, and core covariates (Table G.11); they differ only in how they partition residual variance. We then select among them using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling leave-one-out 63 cross-validation69 (PSIS-LOO; Figure G.9), which balances predictive accuracy against model complexity without requiring held-out test set. The variance decomposition reported in the main text (Figure 3C) and the posterior predictive checks in Figure G.8 are both based on the top-ranked model on the ELPD-LOO frontier. We evaluate the predictive fidelity of the best-fitting latent factor model (M7, see Section G.5.1) using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling (PSIS) to approximate genuine leave-one-out predictions without refitting. In panel (a), LOO-predicted probabilities are grouped by the empirical success rate of each observation; well-calibrated predictions fall on the diagonal, meaning the model assigns 60% probability to observations that succeed 60% of the time, and so on. Panel (b) aggregates predictions to the task level: for each unique task, we average the LOO-predicted and observed success rates across all agent configurations and plot one against the other. The tight clustering 66 around the identity line (R2 > 0.95) confirms that the hierarchical model generalizes beyond in-sample fit, accurately recovering per-task difficulty even after accounting for model abilities, scaffold choice, and tool-description verbosity. Figure G.8: The best-fitting latent factor model is well calibrated and accurately recovers task-level success rates. (a) Leave-one-out (LOO) predicted probabilities grouped by observed success rate (k/n). Each point is one observation, jittered horizontally for visibility; pink bars mark group medians. The dashed diagonal indicates perfect calibration. Median predictions closely track the diagonal across the full range, confirming that the model does not systematically overor under-predict at any difficulty level. (b) Taskaveraged LOO predicted success rate versus observed success rate. Each point represents one task, averaged over all modelscaffoldverbosity configurations. Points cluster tightly around the identity line, indicating that the model captures task-level variation rather than merely fitting marginal statistics. Both panels use M7 (abilities environment scope), the top-ranked model on the ELPD-LOO frontier. Model comparison metrics. We compare candidate models using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling leave-one-out cross-validation (PSIS-LOO).69 The expected log pointwise predictive density (ELPD-LOO) estimates each models out-of-sample predictive accuracy: higher values indicate better generalization. The effective number of parameters (ploo) measures model complexity as the difference between the in-sample and leave-one-out log predictive densities, providing bias-corrected analog of classical parameter counts that accounts for shrinkage in hierarchical models. Together, ELPD-LOO and ploo form predictive-accuracycomplexity trade-off analogous to the information-criterion frontier: models in the upper-left of scatter plot achieve better prediction with less overfitting. Convergence is assessed via the splitˆR statistic, where ˆR < 1.01 across all parameters indicates reliable posterior sampling. Figure G.9 compares complexity against performance. All modeling is implemented in PyMC v570 with inference via the NUTS sampler, and post-processing uses ArviZ [71] for diagnostics and model comparison. Variance decomposition across specifications. Figure G.10 compares the posterior variance budget of all eight candidate models. Two patterns are robust to specification 67 Figure G.9: Ability-based models achieve better predictive accuracy with fewer effective parameters. Scatter plot of leave-one-out expected log pointwise predictive density (ELPD-LOO) against the effective number of parameters (ploo) for eight candidate latent factor models (M1M8). Each marker represents one model specification; color distinguishes models, and the legend reports the maximum ˆR across all parameters as convergence diagnostic. Models in the upper-left corner are preferred: they explain held-out data better (higher ELPD-LOO) while using fewer effective parameters. M7 (abilities + environment + scope) and M3 (abilities + environment) dominate the remaining specifications, indicating that explicit knowledge and reasoning ability terms capture the dominant structure in agent performance. M7 is used in our main text. Only models that converged ( ˆR < 1.01, zero divergences) are included. 68 choice. First, the model-identity component (σmodel, driven by the IRT-derived knowledge and reasoning abilities) is consistently the largest or second-largest contributor, ranging from 31% in the baseline (M1) to 46% in the hybrid (M8). Second, scaffold choice, tool-description verbosity, and task category account for less than 4% combined in every specification. The choice of LLM thus dominates over scaffold configuration. The remaining variance is split between environment-related intercepts and task-specific effects; their relative share depends on the granularity of the grouping structure, but neither overtakes the model component once abilityenvironment interactions are included (M3, M7, M8). Figure G.10: Model identity dominates the variance budget regardless of model specification. Forest plots of the top-five posterior variance-share components for each of the eight latent factor models (M1M8). Dots show the median and bars the 90% credible interval over 8 000 MCMC draws. In simpler specifications (M1, M2, M4, M5) task random effects absorb the largest share (4562%) because environmentand scope-level structure is not modeled explicitly. Once ability slopes are allowed to interact with environment (M3, M7, M8), model identity rises to the dominant component (4446%), while task effects shrink correspondingly. Scaffold, verbosity, and category contribute <4% across all eight specifications. In the best model (see Figure G.11), model identity alone explains 45.2% [41.4, 48.8] of the variance, more than any other source and with credible interval that does not overlap the next-largest contributor (environment scope, 32.5%). Scaffold choice and tool-description verbosity together account for less than 2%. Forest plot of posterior variance shares for the five linearpredictor components of M7. Each row shows the median (dot) and 90% highest-density interval (bar) over all 8 000 MCMC draws. σmodel (knowledge + reasoning ability, indexed by LLM identity) accounts for 45.2% of the explained variance, with credible interval that does not overlap any other component. Environment scope intercepts and task-specific effects contribute most of the remaining variance (32.5% and 20.7%, respectively), while scaffold choice and tool-description verbosity are negligible (<2% combined). across all 16 environment scope cells (range 0.485.76), so higher reasoning ability predicts better task performance regardless of domain or problem scope. G.6 Intervention experiments Protocol. To study whether partial trajectories from prior runs can steer an agent toward recovery or failure, we implement an intervention mechanism. For every (environment, agent, task) triple, we maintain trace registry that maps the triple to two pools of recorded conversation traces: one drawn from successful baseline trials and one from failed baseline trials, selected from tasks with mixed outcomes (success rates between 20% and 80%). Trace materialisation. Given an intervention type τ {success, failed} and step index k, the hook samples trace from the corresponding pool and extracts the subsequence of assistant turns = (a1, . . . , aN ), where each ai usually comprises thoughts and tool invocations. We write ap for the number of assistant turns in trace p. The prefix slice operator selects the turns to inject: slice(a, k) = (a1, . . . , ak) > 0, (a1, . . . , aN +k) < 0, (G1) where positive takes the first steps and negative retains all but the final k. In our experiments we use {1, 2, 2, 1}, probing both early-stage and near-terminal interventions. The sliced steps are appended to the initial task prompt u0 to form the seed history H0. Every tool call is re-executed against the live environment so those observations reflect the true current state. The full procedure is given in Algorithm 1. 71 Algorithm 1 Trace Intervention at Step Require: Trace registry R, intervention type τ , step index k, task id Ensure: Seeded message history H0 (cid:12) (k > 0 ap k) (k < 0 ap > k) (cid:9) 1: R[env, agent, task].τ _traces 2: (cid:8) (cid:12) 3: Uniform(P ) 4: [ Load(p) m.role = "assistant" ] 5: slice(a, k) 6: H0 [u0] 7: for each sj do Append(H0, sj) 8: for each action ParseActions(sj) do ExecuteTool(c.name, c.args) Append(H0, Observation(o)) 9: 10: 11: end for 12: 13: end for Look up trace pool Sample one trace per trial Eq. G1 Task prompt Thought + action 14: return H0 Agent continues from here The agent resumes auto-regressively from H0, unaware that the initial turns were replayed. Controls. Each task is run under three regimes: baseline (no intervention), success intervention, and failed intervention. We run 15 trials per condition, yielding Pass@k and Passk metrics for = 1, . . . , 15. Failed-trace injections serve as negative control: if injecting successful reasoning genuinely aids recovery, injecting failed reasoning should not confer the same benefit and may hinder performance. different trace is sampled uniformly from the pool for each trial, mitigating single-trace confounds. All trials use temperature of 0.7. Per-environment recovery curves are shown in Figure G.12, and Pass@k decay by scaffold in Figure G.13. 72 Figure G.12: Recovery curves under success and failed trace interventions across environments. Top two rows show agent success rates when injecting steps from successful trajectory; bottom two rows show the same for failed trajectory. Environments exhibit two distinct recovery profiles. Hypothesis-driven inquiry environments (Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation, Inorganic Qualitative Analysis) show minimal benefit from early successful stepsoften at or below baselinefollowed by sharp jump when later steps are injected (Step n2, Step n1). Conversely, injecting late steps from failed trace is catastrophic in these environments, dropping success rates to near zero (0.12), indicating that erroneous reasoning strongly anchors the agent. In contrast, Workflow construction environments (ML-based Property Prediction) exhibit monotonic, gradual improvement from early steps onward, and are more robust to failed traces (success rates remain 0.40). Dashed lines denote the tool-calling agent; solid lines denote ReAct. 73 Figure G.13: Pass@k decay under trace interventions across scaffold architectures. Pass@k as function of under trace interventions, shown separately for ReAct and tool-calling agents. Rows correspond to successful interventions (top two) and failed interventions (bottom two), with columns representing each environment. The grey baseline curve is repeated in each panel for reference. Marker opacity decreases with distance from the intervention point (Step 1 darkest, Step n2 lightest). G.7 Token-level log-probability analysis We use mean token-level log-probabilities (Section 4.10) as an environment-level proxy for model uncertainty. For each environment, we pool the log-probability of the top-1 token across all assistant messages and trials, excluding special control tokens, and report the perenvironment mean ℓe (Figure G.14). Workflow-construction domains (ML-based Property 74 Figure G.14: Mean log probability assigned by GPT-OSS-120B averaged over all tokens in completions across all traces for each environment type. Hypothesisdriven environments yield consistently lower mean log probability than workflow-based environments. Prediction, Adsorption Surface Construction, Molecular Simulation) exhibit the least negative log-probabilities (ℓe 0.22), indicating high token-level confidence consistent with well-defined solution paths. Hypothesis-driven and strategic-reasoning domains (Inorganic Qualitative Analysis,Spectroscopic Structure Elucidation,Retrosynthetic Planning) share the most negative values (ℓe = 0.27). This ordering mirrors the gradient in epistemic demand described in the main text and aligns with the trace-intervention finding that near-complete trajectories are required to improve performance in exactly those environments where token-level confidence is lowest (Figure 5A). G.8 Verbosity detailed results We compare agent scores at the three tool-documentation verbosity levels defined in Section 4.2, holding the model, scaffold, environment, and scope fixed. Scores are aggregated per environment, per model, and per scaffold. Figure G.15 shows that verbosity has negligible effect, consistent with the <0.1% variance share attributed to verbosity by the latent factor model (Figure 3C). Figure G.15: Detailed results across tool-documentation verbosity levels. (A) Results across environments. Scores are close across verbosity levels. (B) Change in workflow and comprehensive scores relative to brief. Differences are within 0.05 points in every environment. (C) Results per model. Each models scores are close across the three verbosity levels. (D) Results per agent. Scores for the two scaffolds track each other across verbosity levels.

sections.title: H Reasoning process analysis
sections.content: This analysis examines how agents reach their answers, independent of whether the answer is correct. Each reasoning trace is annotated as graph of epistemic operations (Section 4.8); productive patterns and reasoning breakdowns are detected as structural templates over the graph. Annotation and review. The two-stage LLM annotation pipeline is described in Section 4.8 and expanded with implementation details in Section H.1. Manual review by two domain-expert annotators was performed on 626 selected traces to quantify agreement with the LLM pipeline. detailed analysis of the annotation results can be found in Section H.5. 76 Pattern prevalence across models. Reasoning breakdowns dominate in both annotated models. Evidence non-uptake appears in 88% of traces for both Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT4o , and untested claims in 66% and 62% respectively (Table H.16). Productive counterparts to these breakdowns are rare: convergent multi-test evidence and evidence-guided test redesign each appear in at most 1% of traces; refutation-driven belief revision appears in 4% and 1%. The two models diverge most on fixed belief traces (47% versus 67%) and on stalled revision (21% versus 8%); elsewhere their profiles are close. Pattern prevalence across environments. Figure H.17 shows that reasoning breakdowns exceed productive motifs at every environment level. This is consistent with the main-text claim that undisciplined reasoning appears across task types rather than emerging only under the highest task difficulty. H.1 LLM annotation pipeline The epistemological graph prevalences summarised in Table H.16 and Figure H.17 are produced by two-stage annotation pipeline operating on the serialized message history of each completed agent run. Input representation. Each trace is serialized to linear message list with role, content, and step index preserved. System prompts and iteration-limit error messages are excluded. Task descriptions remain eligible for annotation because they carry information the agent subsequently uses. Stage 1: node labeling. Claude Sonnet 4.5 is prompted to label message-level nodes with epistemic operations drawn from the fixed vocabulary {hypothesis, test, evidence, judgment, commitment, final answer}. Annotation proceeds in overlapping windows of 20 messages with stride of 15, so each message appears in several windows. For each eligible node, the model returns label, supporting quote taken verbatim from the message, and short gloss. Decoding uses temperature 0.7. After all windows are processed, nodes referring to the same message are merged and duplicates removed. Tool responses are assigned as evidence. Stage 2: edge construction. The labeled graph and the original messages are passed to second Claude Sonnet 4.5 call, using the same windowing as in stage 1. The call adds directed edges between related nodes, typed from the fixed vocabulary {testing, observing, informing, contradicting, competing, updating}. Each edge carries supporting quote and the source-message index. Decoding uses temperature 0.7. In the pilot prompt-development experiments (see Section H.2), Claude Sonnet 4.5 provided the best balance between annotation quality and cost, and showed the strongest overall performance in schema adherence, quote grounding, and consistency across long traces, as judged by the experts reviewing the annotations. Validation Every graph undergoes three automated checks: (i) every supporting quote is checked for verbatim substring membership in the cited message. Under the default mode, failures are logged as warnings, and the item is retained; (ii) each edge must connect nodes that exist in the graph, and (iii) only permitted edge-node combinations are allowed (see Appendix H.2). Graphs failing any of these checks are discarded, and warning is raised. Table H.13 shows that most validation warnings arise from non-verbatim quote mismatches rather than structural problems. Edge-stage quote mismatches account for 56.0% of all warnings, and node-stage mismatches for 25.8%, together over 80% of flagged cases. Disallowed edge-node combinations are less frequent (11.1%). Corrections on observation messages (extra nodes and type changes) and other structural fixes are rare. No schema violations were observed for any model; the constrained-generation setup reliably enforced the node and edge vocabularies. 78 Table H.13: Quality-control warning counts by failure mode and model. Each annotated reasoning graph was automatically validated after generation. Non-verbatim quote warnings occur when the cited evidence excerpt for node (node stage) or an edge (edge stage) is not an exact substring of the source trace; such annotations are retained but flagged. Disallowed combination denotes an edge whose relationtype triple falls outside the permitted schema vocabulary. Extra node at observation message counts evidence/claim nodes that were injected into observation-only messages and subsequently removed. Node type corrected to enforce E-only rule counts nodes whose type was automatically changed to enforce the constraint that observation messages may contain only Evidence nodes. Other captures miscellaneous structural corrections (e.g., node additions or residual type fixes). Schema violations (invalid node types or edge relations outside the vocabulary) were never observed. Percentages are computed over the total number of warnings across all models. Failure mode Claude Sonnet 4.5 GPT-4 GPT-OSS-120B Non-verbatim quote (node stage) Non-verbatim quote (edge stage) Disallowed (relation, src, dst) combination Extra node at observation message (removed) Node type corrected to enforce E-only rule ad- (node Other ditions type / corrections) Schema (none observed) violations 4,166 9, 1,402 769 190 117 0 1, 3,999 1,103 403 102 111 388 967 317 65 0 0 Total 6,549 % 25.8 14, 56.0 2,822 11.1 1,237 4.9 269 1.2 1.1 0 0.

sections.title: Total
sections.content: 15,878 7,713 1,778 25,369 100.0 H.2 Annotations prompts The annotation pipeline comprises two sequential passes, each consisting of system prompt followed by user instruction prompt. The system prompts define the models behavioral constraints and high-level rules. Both prompts conclude with an explicit directive to produce JSON that conforms to the required schema, without any surrounding prose or explanation. 79 System prompt for Stage 1: Node annotation You are careful annotator. You MUST only extract information explicitly present in the provided messages. Rules: - Do NOT invent hidden thoughts or implied steps. - Do not borrow any external knowledge or make assumptions beyond the text. - Do not judge or correct the content, only label supported nodes. - For messages with several nodes, you MUST follow the order in which they appear in the text to assign message indices. - If uncertain, omit the node rather than guessing. - Output JSON only, matching the required schema. User prompts for Stage 1 annotation Extract 0..k nodes from the provided message window. Every non-Observation message must be assigned with at least one node. It is possible that some messages will have multiple nodes (e.g., message that both states hypothesis and describes test). Avoid repeated nodes of the **same type** within single message, unless the text explicitly supports multiple distinct instances. Node types: = Hypothesis: candidate explanation, or working assumption about the system. It should be revisable claim, proposal, suggestion, or the current best guess about the answer. Information in task definitions or environment descriptions do not count as hypotheses (H). Partial or full reiteration of the task descriptionn does not count as hypothesis (H). Correcting typo in tool argument does not count as Hypothesis (H). We consider Hypothesis (H) to be present if the agent states claim similar to "I think the answer is X", "This suggests that might be the solution", "The most likely explanation is X", "X could be the case if...", "It seems that the answer can be X", etc. = Evidence: all Observation messages must be assigned with and only with an Evidence (E) node. Only Observation messages can be Evidence (E) nodes, with the exception of the task description message. = Neutral: for boilerplate operations like writing or copying files, or non-scientific tool calls. Only the tool calls of message can be assigned as Neutral (N). = Test: any information-seeking action, including experiments, evaluations, or lookups. Both the intention and the concrete tool call qualify as tests (T); what matters is that the system is seeking new scientific information to evaluate hypothesis (H) or judgment (J). If both the intention/plan to run test and the actual tool call are 80 present within the same message, count only the tool call as the test (T) node. Every tool call (with the tag <action>) must be assigned as either Test (T) or Neutral (N) node. = Judgment: an interpretation of test results (Observation) that goes beyond the literal repetition of the raw output. If the agent restates an observation while adding any evaluative, comparative, or inferential content, even brief it is judgment (J). = Final Answer: Only the message that contains final submisssion (with tag <final_answer>), must be assigned with Final Answer (F) node. Constraints for nodes: - Only label what is explicitly present in text. - Every node must have support quotes (exact substrings) and msg indices. - If you normalize text, still cite original quote(s). Pseudo-nodes (not explicitly stated but can be inferred): = Commitment: if from the actions of the agents or system it can be inferred that they have reached an implicit commitment to an answer that is not yet fully supported by evidence, and that they are **refusing to revise it**, then create pseudo-node labeled C. This is special pseudo-node that captures the commitment even if it is not explicitly stated. It is possible that the Final Answer (F) node is also Commitment (C) if the Final Answer (F) is not fully supported by evidence. Constrains for pseudo-nodes: - Only Commitment (C) can be pseudo-node. - For the quote support of pseudo-node, you can cite the text that implies the commitment, even if it is not explicit. You are allowed to add brief explanation to clarify the implication, but it must be concise and directly tied to the quote. Return JSON with keys: "nodes": [ "node_id": "N1", "type": "HTEJCF", "time": <int message index of earliest support>, "text": <normalized short node text>, "support": ["msg_idx": <int>, "quote": <exact substring from that message>], , ... ] 81 In the second pass (Stage 2), the model receives the previously extracted nodes alongside the same message window. Its task is to identify directed edges between those nodes. The user prompt enumerates all permissible relation types and, for each type, specifies the valid sourcedestination pair combinations. This ensures that only structurally grounded edges are extracted. Edges that are not permitted are deleted programmatically after annotation. System prompt for Stage 2: Edge annotation You are careful annotator. You MUST only add edges supported by explicit text. Rules: - You may only connect nodes provided to you. - Do not borrow any external knowledge or make assumptions beyond the text. - Do not judge or correct the content, only label supported edges. - Every edge MUST include at least one support quote with message indices. - If uncertain, omit the edge rather than guessing. - Output JSON only, matching the required schema. User prompts for Stage 2 annotation Given the message window and list of extracted nodes (with node_id and text), extract supported edges among these nodes. Only the following edge types are allowed, any other combination is forbidden: - tests: Allowed only between: -> T, -> T. -> T: the Test (T) directly addresses the Hypothesis claim (H), or attempts to falsify or verify it. -> T: the Test (T) is designed in response to the Judgment (J), or the Judgment (J) motivates the test design. - observes: Allowed only between: -> E. -> E: the Evidence (E) is direct result of the Test (T). - updates_to: Allowed only between: -> H. -> H: the later Hypothesis (H) is revision of the earlier one, based on the nodes in between. - competes_with: Allowed only between: -> H. -> H: the two Hypotheses (H) are alternative explanations that are directly compared or evaluated against each other. Both hypotheses should be plausible and co-existing at the same time. If another hypothesis is introduced later as revision of the first one, then it should be connected with updates_to instead of competes_with. - contradicts: Allowed only between: -> H, -> H. -> H: the Evidence (E) contradicts the claim of the Hypothesis (H). -> H: the Judgment (J) contradicts the claim of the Hypothesis (H). - informs: Allowed only between: -> H, -> J, -> C, -> C, -> H, -> J. -> H: the Evidence (E) provides information relevant to the claim of the Hypothesis (H). -> J: the Judgment (J) is an interpretation of the Evidence (E). -> C: the Commitment (C) is informed by the Evidence (E) but not necessarily in an explicit way. -> H: the Judgment (J) provides information relevant to the claim of the Hypothesis (H). -> J: the later Judgment (J) is refinement, an extension, or combination of one or serveral earlier judgments (J). -> C: the Commitment (C) is informed by the Judgment (J) but not necessarily in an explicit way. Return JSON with keys: "edges": [ "src": "<node_id>", "dst": "<node_id>", "relation": "<one of allowed>", "time": <int message index of earliest support>, "support": ["msg_idx": <int>, "quote": <exact substring from that message>] , ... ] Prompt optimization Two experts optimized the annotation prompts for both stages by defining node and edge definitions in an iterative process on 50-trace training set. The experts manually reviewed the LLMs annotations and suggested improvements. When both agreed that the annotations reliably captured the underlying reasoning patterns, they proceeded with the full annotation. Effect of prompt optimization. Prompt optimization led to substantial improvements in annotation agreement (Figure H.16). Human-human agreement measured by PABAK increased from 85.8% to 92.6%, while human-LLM percent agreement rose from 89.3% to 95.7%. The gains were similar for node and edge annotations. 83 Figure H.16: Effect of prompt optimization on annotation agreement. Human-human (PABAK) and human-LLM (percent agreement) scores for node and edge annotations, comparing the initial round (old) and the optimized round (new). H.3 Pattern taxonomies Productive motifs and reasoning breakdowns are defined as explicit structural templates over the annotated graphs. Each template specifies the required node types, edge types, and their configuration; traces are scored by detecting instances of these templates. Templates are organized into three families. Hypothesis handling captures how candidate explanations are generated, weighed against evidence, and revised. Evidence handling captures how the results of tests are incorporated into belief. Inquiry control captures how the overall test strategy is planned and adjusted. Productive motifs. Productive motifs are drawn from recognized practices of disciplined inquiry: Popperian falsification,56 exploratory-to-confirmatory transitions,57 triangulation by convergent multi-test evidence, and active-learning-style test redesign.58,59 Table H.14 lists every template with its short description and graph form. Table H.14: Descriptions and graph showing the productive motifs in the epistemological analysis. Note that data-first hypothesis and iterative test refinement include two graphs each that refer to the same productive reasoning behavior.

Reasoning breakdowns are drawn from established work on scientific-reasoning failure and from patterns observed during trace review. Hypothesishandling breakdowns include untested claims, contradictions left without repair, and premature commitment. Evidence-handling breakdowns include evidence non-uptake, disconnected evidence, and unsupported judgment. Inquiry-control breakdowns include fixed belief traces, stalled revision, and fixed hypothesis test tuning (ad-hoc rescue in the Popperian sense). Both capture configurations in which the agent does not revise its plan in response to new information. Figure H.17 breaks down the same prevalence estimates by environment and subtask level, aggregating across models. Each row represents one environment (e.g., Spectroscopic structure elucidation S1) and is color-coded according to 88 its category among the three environment types: workflow execution, strategic reasoning, and hypothesis-driven inquiry. Across every environment level, reasoning breakdowns consistently outnumber productive motifs. Hypothesis-driven environments show the highest overall annotation density, which aligns with their greater demands for iterative belief revision. Figure H.17: Motif mean prevalence detailed for each environment. Values are averaged across the corresponding annotated pattern sets and aggregated across models. Reasoning breakdowns are more prevalent than productive motifs across all environment scopes. Scope invariance across subtask levels. Figure H.18 examines how epistemic operation prevalence changes as problem scope increases from S1 to S4, averaging across all multilevel environments. Despite the fourfold increase in task complexity, neither the absolute prevalence nor the relative ordering of these operations shifts systematically. This invariance suggests that agents do not adapt their epistemic behavior to broader or more complex problem settings. 89 Figure H.18: Epistemic operation prevalence remains invariant to increasing problem scope. Mean prevalence of productive motifs (solid lines) and reasoning breakdowns (dashed lines) across the three epistemic groups (hypothesis handling, evidence handling, and inquiry control), plotted against increasing problem scope (S1S4), averaged over all multi-level environments. Error bars denote the standard error of the mean across environments. Despite fourfold increase in task complexity, neither the absolute prevalence nor the relative ordering of epistemic operations shifts systematically, indicating that agents apply fixed repertoire of reasoning strategies regardless of problem scope. H.5 Annotation agreement analysis To validate the automated LLM annotation pipeline, three domain-expert annotators independently reviewed representative sample of 25 traces drawn from the full set of 626 traces, covering both annotation rounds (before and after prompt optimization). We report inter-annotator agreement at two levels: humanhuman and humanLLM. Because the label distribution is heavily skewed toward correct, which deflates Cohens κ, we additionally report the prevalenceand bias-adjusted kappa (PABAK) as more informative chance-corrected measure. Humanhuman agreement. Table H.17 summarizes pairwise agreement among the three annotators on the optimized prompt annotations. Overall percent agreement reaches 92.6%, with mean PABAK of 0.853, indicating substantial agreement. Node-level annotations show higher concordance (PABAK up to 0.976) than edge-level ones (PABAK 0.7370.913); this is expected, as judging relational edges requires more nuanced interpretation of the trace. Cohens κ values are low (overall 0.067) due to the extreme prevalence of the correct class. PABAK corrects for this imbalance and confirms that observed disagreements are rare. 90 Table H.17: Humanhuman inter-annotator agreement on optimized annotations. Pairwise Cohens κ, PABAK, and percent agreement for nodes and edges across three domain experts. Low κ values reflect the high prevalence of the correct class; PABAK corrects for this imbalance.

Specifically, we examine the distribution of tool actions, the verbosity of model outputs, and the number of tool calls per trial. These analyses help clarify whether differences in task performance stem from distinct interaction strategies or from more subtle variations in how models use the same tool ecosystem. Figures H.19 and H.20 summarize these behavioral patterns across environments, agent scaffolds, and model families. Figure H.19 shows the distribution of action categories across environments, split by agent type and by model. The two agent scaffolds exhibit similar action profiles, suggesting that ReAct and tool-calling agents rely on comparable tool-use patterns. In contrast, differences across models are more pronounced in specific environments, indicating that the model family has stronger influence than the agent scaffold on which types of tools are invoked. 92 Figure H.19: Detailed results of action distributions in Corral. Tools are classified according to the task type they perform. (A) Action distribution percentages by agent type. Differences between agents are minor, with both showing similar tool use patterns across environments. (B) Action distribution percentages by model type. Notable differences across models emerge in specific environments: only GPT-4o employs code execution tools in the Resistor environment. In AFM, both Claude and GPT-4o use these tools substantially more than GPT-Oss, whereas in MD, Claude shows the highest usage among the three models. Figure H.20 provides complementary view based on output length and tool-use intensity. The figure shows that verbosity varies substantially across environments (some settings elicit longer responses than others) and that models also differ in how many tokens they produce and how many tools they call during trial. The behavioral differences in this figure are stronger than those in the action-mixture analysis, suggesting that models differ less in what types of actions they take than in how extensively they reason and interact with tools while solving the task. Resistor yields markedly more tokens per message than the other environments. Environments with lower performance tend to produce higher number of tokens. (B) Tokens per message for each agent. Despite ReAct explicitly incorporating Thought category to prompt reasoning, the token distributions across agents are similar. (C) Tokens per message for each model. GPT-Oss is the most verbose model, most likely due to its use of reasoning tokens. (D) Tool calls per trial across environments. Average number of tool calls per trial, aggregated over models and agents. (E) Tool distribution for each agent. Both agents exhibit similar tool-call distributions. Although the tool-calling agent shows slightly longer tail, the overall differences are minimal. (F) Number of tool calls per model. The 20 candidate structures include the correct answer, yet node N14 has zero outgoing informs edges: the isomer list is never used to constrain any judgment or hypothesis. The agent instead guesses structures one by one (phenanthrenecarboxylate, anthracenecarboxylate, . . . ) without consulting the evidence it already obtained. 99 Untested claim Model: Claude Sonnet 4.5 Environment: NMR structure elucidation (spectra) Scope: 1 Trial: 22 Msg 3 (Observation) proton_nmr_spectra Deltas 2.23 (s, 3H), 2.81 (t, = 7.1 Hz, 2H), 2.98 (t, = 7.1 Hz, 2H), 7.17 (ddd, 7H), 7.90 (ddd, 2H). The aromatic signals suggest substituted benzene ring. The singlet at 2.23 ppm suggests methyl group attached to carbonyl (acetyl group). calls mass_spectrometry_spectra N2 informs substituted benzene ring N3 Two coupled CH2 groups; singlet at 2.23 ppm suggests acetyl group; Msg 5 (Observation) mass_spectrometry_spectra m/z 224.12 (intensity 100) . . . The agent moves directly to mass spectrometry: no test is designed to evaluate the CH2-CH2 or acetyl-group hypothesis. N2 informs tests N3 Annotator quote: The two triplets at 2.81 and 2.98 ppm suggest two CH2 groups coupled to each other (likely -CH2-CH2-). The aromatic signals suggest substituted benzene ring. The singlet at 2.23 ppm suggests methyl group attached to carbonyl (acetyl group). Node N3 carries detailed structural interpretation but has zero outgoing tests edges. The agent asserts the presence of an acetyl group and coupled CH2 groups as fact, then immediately requests mass spectrometry to determine molecular weight rather than designing an experiment (e.g., simulating 13C NMR for candidate) to verify the claim. 100 Fixed belief trace Model: GPT-4o Environment: LAMMPS molecular dynamics (md) Scope: 2 Trial: 33 Msg 15 (Observation) list_files {files: [/potentials/BKS, /potentials/EAM, /potentials/SW, /potentials/TERSOFF]} N10 List files in /potentials/ to find correct SW potential path directory contains BKS, EAM, SW, TERSOFF subdirectories observes N11 Potentials Msg 16 (Agent) The /potentials/ directory contains file named SW, which likely corresponds to the Stillinger-Weber potential. will update the LAMMPS input script to use this file . . . calls write_file to set pair_coeff * * /potentials/SW Si N11 informs ... three error messages and re-writes later N12 The SW potential file is located at /potentials/SW Msg 27 (Observation) run_lammps ERROR: Lost atoms: original 8 current 0 N33 Run LAMMPS simulation with updated script using /potentials/SW/Si.sw N34 LAMMPS simulation failed: Lost atoms observes Msg 28 (Agent) This might be due to the rapid heating rate or insufficient equilibration. will adjust the heating rate and ensure proper equilibration . . . N34 (no informs to any existing H) N35 Lost atoms error may be due to rapid heating rate or insufficient equilibration updates_to N19 N46 updates_to N57 Annotator quotes: N12 (msg 16): The /potentials/ directory contains file named SW, which likely corresponds to the Stillinger-Weber potential. N35 (msg 28): This might be due to the rapid heating rate or insufficient equilibration. The trace contains 6 hypothesis nodes and zero updates_to edges. The first hypothesis (N12) mistakes the directory /potentials/SW for file, producing an error that is never traced back to the belief. After two more failures, the agent introduces second independent hypothesis (N35), attributing the lost atoms to heating rate, without revising the earlier structural error. Both beliefs persist unmodified through the remaining 15 messages. 101 Contradiction without repair Model: Claude Sonnet 4.5 Environment: NMR structure elucidation (spectra) Scope: 2 Trial: 45 Msg 17 (Observation) simulate_spectra for CC(C)OC(=O)c1ccccc1N(C)C 1H NMR: 1.46 (d, 6H), 2.97 (s, 6H), 4.75 (p, 1H), aromatic protons. N31 Simulate spectra for CC(C)OC(=O)c1ccccc1N(C)C 1.46 ppm (d, 6H); experimental: 1.43 ppm (d, 3H) observes N25 Simulated: Msg 18 (Agent) H-NMR: 1.46 ppm (d, 6H) This is for isopropyl CH3 groups, but experimental shows 1.43 ppm (d, 3H). The experimental data has only 3H in the doublet, not 6H. So this isnt isopropyl ester. The experimental data clearly shows CH(CH3), not CH(CH3)2. calls get_formula_from_smiles N25 informs experimental shows only 3H doublet at 1.43 ppm N33 contradicts ester N30 The compound is 2-(dimethylamino)benzoic acid isopropyl N33 Simulated isopropyl ester shows 6H doublet at 1.46 ppm, but Msg 20 (Agent final answer) Simulations are approximations and can have errors . . . The key evidence: (1) quartet at 5.05 ppm, (2) singlet at 2.69 ppm (6H) = N(CH3)2, (3) 4 aromatic protons, (4) carbonyl at 168.30 ppm. <final_answer>CC(C)OC(=O)c1ccccc1N(C)C</final_answer> N36 Despite the 3H vs. 6H discrepancy, the overall evidence supports the isopropyl ester N37 Agent commits despite unresolved discrepancy informs N33 contradicts updates_to N30 Annotator quote: H-NMR: 1.46 ppm (d, 6H) This is for isopropyl CH3 groups, but experimental shows 1.43 ppm (d, 3H) . . . The experimental data has only 3H in the doublet, not 6H. So this isnt isopropyl ester. The agent explicitly notes that the simulated 1H NMR contradicts its own hypothesis (N30). Yet no revised hypothesis is generated: the contradicts edge from N33 to N30 has no accompanying updates_to edge. Instead, the agent dismisses the contradiction as simulation artifact and submits the isopropyl ester (CC(C)OC(=O)c1ccccc1N(C)C) as the final answer (N38).

sections.title: I Scope and limitations
sections.content: The results reported here are bounded by deliberate design choices and by practical constraints on model access and compute. We state both so that comparisons to future work, whether with richer scaffolds, broader model coverage, or larger compute budgets, can be made on equal terms. Scope of the evaluation. Each task is treated as an independent episode: agents receive no carry-over of knowledge, strategies, or refined heuristics across tasks, in contrast with the iterative accumulation of intuition that characterizes real scientific work. We evaluate two simple scaffolds, ReAct and native tool calling, with flat, append-only conversation history and simple truncation heuristic (earlier non-assistant messages shortened to 100 characters) when the context window is exceeded. Advanced orchestration is outside the scope of this evaluation: plan-then-execute pipelines, hierarchical planners, explicit summarisation or retrieval, and multi-agent architectures such as debate or specialist delegation. This isolates the contribution of the base model and scaffold from engineeringintensive orchestration; the scores reported here, therefore, bound what minimal scaffolds achieve rather than what is achievable in general. All agents operate under fixed budget of 2040 LLM calls per task, calibrated in pilot runs to be sufficient for task completion, and at fixed sampling temperature of 0.0. We do not vary this budget per task nor conduct temperature sensitivity analysis. Model coverage and compute. Our evaluation spans three models, Claude Sonnet 4.5 , GPT-4o , and GPT-OSS (gpt-oss-120b), selected to span proprietary and open-weight systems at the current capability frontier; other prominent families are omitted. The full benchmark consumed approximately 3 billion tokens across 138 configuration-environment pairs at five trials each, with an estimated API cost of $7.5k for the two proprietary models (excluding infrastructure for the self-hosted open-weight model) for the main final runs shown in the main text (not including interventions and subtasks). Pass@k metrics account for stochastic variation, but scaling to additional models, trials, or environments carries non-trivial cost. Measurement caveats. Log-probability-based analyses of model confidence and calibration are restricted to GPT-OSS (gpt-oss-120b), as proprietary API providers do not expose per-token log-probabilities for tool-calling or for sufficiently long completions. We also observed higher rates of malformed responses from GPT-OSS-120B (41% of traces affected, with 1.26 errors per trace), and to lesser extent GPT-4o (0.28 errors per trial, affecting 6.2% of traces), compared with Claude Sonnet 4.5 (0.5% of traces affected); the most common failure modes were invalid JSON in tool arguments and missing XML closing tags in ReAct traces. We did not apply engineering mitigations such as constrained decoding, output grammars, or retry-with-repair loops. The reported numbers thus reflect the behavior 103 of each system under the same minimal scaffold, not their best-case performance with model-specific hardening.
